title: NLESO-Based High-Fidelity Reconstruction for Noisy Magnetic Grating Signals
authors: Yifei Bai, Deren Kong
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: In artillery recoil testing, conventional filters compromise phase integrity for noise suppression, leading to delayed peaks and erroneous velocity calculations from magnetic grating sensor signals. This paper presents a high-fidelity, zero-phase reconstruction method by applying a Nonlinear Extended State Observer (NLESO) to this extreme dynamic engineering challenge. The NLESO leverages adaptive nonlinear feedback to effectively attenuate intense high-frequency noise without introducing phase lag. Validated on both generic transient signals and real-world 10 kHz artillery recoil data, the proposed method achieves near-zero delay, the lowest RMSE, and enables direct extraction of smooth, high-quality recoil velocity. This work provides a robust, real-time capable solution for accurate displacement and velocity measurement in harsh shock environments.

title: A Privacy-Aware Medical Record Query Scheme with Anonymous Authentication in Wireless Body Area Networks
authors: Yuqi Xie, Tao Zhao, Tu Peng, Ling Xiong, Zhicai Liu, Neal Xiong
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: With the integration of intelligent medical devices and wireless sensor networks, telemedicine queries have significantly enhanced the efficiency and accessibility of medical services. However, the security and privacy of medical query records remain a critical challenge. While existing privacy-preserving query frameworks in Wireless Body Area Networks (WBANs) primarily focus on protecting the privacy of medical records, they often lack robust mechanisms for authenticating query users. This deficiency may enable malicious entities to gain unauthorized access, thereby resulting in data leakage. To address this issue, we propose a privacy-preserving query scheme with unlinkability by integrating double-Schnorr-based anonymous authentication and 1-out-of-n oblivious transfer (OT). Specifically, anonymous authentication is achieved via a double-Schnorr-based credential construction with session-dependent pseudonym blinding, enabling the server to verify user legitimacy without revealing users’ real identities. OT is implemented through a short-hash-based keyword partition mechanism and masked encryption, ensuring that the curious server cannot infer user queries while fulfilling them correctly. Additionally, query privileges are restricted to legitimate users by verifying anonymous credentials issued during the registration phase. The formal security analysis confirms that the proposed scheme achieves essential security attributes, including anonymous authentication, query privacy, and unlinkability. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed scheme not only integrates anonymous authentication but also outperforms baseline schemes in both computational and communication efficiency. Therefore, the proposed scheme is more suitable for real-world deployment within IoT-based healthcare ecosystems.

title: Editorial Special Issue on Interactive Power Converters for Renewable Energy Grid-Tied Systems—Part II
authors: Georgios Konstantinou, Giovanni de Carne, Marta Molinas
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics

title: A Versatile Analytic Phase Noise Model for Optical Frequency-Modulated Continuous-Wave Interferometry
authors: Xiang Zheng, Yi Dong, Ling Zhang, Qiang Yang, Bowen Li, Congfan Wang, Xin Li, Wei Wei, Weilin Xie
publish: Journal of Lightwave Technology
abstract: Frequency noise of frequency-swept laser fundamentally governs the performance of optical frequency-modulated continuous-wave (OFMCW) interferometry based technologies. Here, we proposed general and versatile analytic model capable of throughout analyzing and accurately predicting the performance of OFMCW system for lasers without assuming specific analytical forms of frequency noise spectrum (FNS). Compared to conventional noise models that rely on specific analytical expression or empirical parameter fitting, this approach establishes a direct link between the laser FNS and the performance of OFMCW system. It allows for highly accurate prediction of the beat note spectrum regardless of laser FNS characteristics with a computational complexity of O(NlogN), and thus enables assessing the impact of laser FNS on key performance metrics such as carrier-to-noise ratio, effective distance, and spatial resolution. Both theoretical and experimental demonstrations for laser with irregular FNS by incorporating optical phase-locked loop (OPLL) have validated an excellent agreement between the predictions and actual measurements with an average R-square of 0.9910. For a practical usage, the analysis permitted by this model reveals the dominance of low-frequency noise, confirming the important role of OPLL in improving the performance. With the verified accuracy, versatility, efficiency, and reliability, the proposed model provides a comprehensive and practical tool for design, characterization, and optimization of OFMCW systems.

title: Identification of Hail and Size Estimation using Weather Radar Spectral Reflectivity: Insights from the RELAMPAGO Field Campaign
authors: Aiswarya Lakshmi K K, Swaroop Sahoo, V. Chandrasekar
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Dual-polarization Doppler weather radars operating at S- and C-bands are proven tools for hail detection. However, this research focuses on using C-band spectral polarimetric variables observations to refine the identification of hail signatures as well as the determination of maximum probable hail sizes in convective storms. In this context, a retrieval algorithm has been introduced that minimizes the difference between measured and modeled spectral reflectivity. The spectral reflectivity model is formulated using hailstone terminal fall velocity-diameter relation, scattering cross-sections computed using T-matrix, and the information on effective spectral broadening. The retrieval algorithm uses C-band radar measurements from CSU-CHIVO, a dual-polarization Doppler weather radar deployed during the RELAMPAGO field campaign in Argentina. The algorithm also uses a combination of radiosonde and ERA5 reanalysis data to determine the vertical Doppler velocity range for the reflectivity spectrum as well as estimate the vertical distribution of hail sizes in a convective storm. The hail size distribution at various altitudes has been found to be primarily in the range of 2 to 14 cm during convective storms, with varying mean diameters. The uncertainty associated with this distribution is 0.4 to 1.6 cm if the uncertainty for Doppler velocity is 2-4 m/s. The maximum probable hail size has been found to be 5 cm, with 95% confidence intervals ranging from 3 to 10 cm, confirming the dominance of hail sizes <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$>2.5$</tex-math></inline-formula> cm. The sensitivity analysis of the retrieval algorithm shows that it is sensitive variations in measurements, backscatter cross-section, vertical wind velocity and turbulence. The results highlight spectral reflectivity coupled with a physics-based retrieval algorithm as a robust framework for quantifying hail size and microphysical properties.

title: Trust Region Search-Based Bayesian Safety Evaluation Method for Accelerated Testing of Autonomous Vehicles
authors: Hao Wu, Yan Wu, Lifang Wang, Junzhi Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Conducting efficient and accurate safety evaluations within the context of naturalistic driving environments is critical in the design and large-scale deployment of Autonomous Vehicles (AV). However, existing closed box safety evaluation algorithms suffer from inefficiency and are challenged to achieve rapid exploration, precise identification of safety boundaries, and accurate estimation of the AV’s failure probability simultaneously. To address these issues, we propose a Trust Region Search-based Bayesian Safety Evaluation (TuRS-BSE) algorithm. It combines Gaussian Processes (GP), the logit function, and the logistic function to construct a surrogate model for the AV, enabling the prediction of test results under different test scenarios. It also introduces three acquisition functions to enable efficient and comprehensive exploration, precise identification of safety boundaries, and the generation of risk scenarios for the AV. Meanwhile, the Trust Region Search (TuRS) algorithm is used to solve for the optimal candidate test scenario iteratively, enhancing the search efficiency. Additionally, the innovative integration of likelihood weighting with an efficient surrogate model eliminates the dependency on parameterized proposal distributions, enabling efficient and accurate estimation of the AV’s failure probability. To validate the effectiveness of the proposed method, we evaluated the AV system in both 2-dimension and 4-dimension test scenarios. The results demonstrated that the proposed approach can get an unbiased estimation of the failure probability and improve the efficiency of safety evaluation.

title: Coherence-Based Inversion of Pulse-Echo Data for Ocean Sound Speed Estimation
authors: Mohammad Reza Mousavi, Len Zedel
publish: IEEE Journal of Oceanic Engineering
abstract: Ocean sound speed profile measurement is critical for marine applications, yet current methods necessitate direct sensor deployment. This article introduces a method for remote ocean sound speed measurement inspired by techniques used in medical ultrasound. A single acoustic transmitter projects sound, and a linear array of receivers is used to record sound reflected by naturally occurring targets. An inversion approach is used to optimize coherence among the received signals by searching for trial average sound speed and scatterer locations. For each scatterer location, the average sound speed maximizing the signal alignment is considered as the estimation. The method's effectiveness is validated using a ray acoustic propagation model. We analyze system performance subject to system parameters, including array length, number of receivers, and pulse characteristics, alongside environmental conditions. Compared to medical ultrasound, the ocean environment has several key advantages, including horizontal stratification, the presence of distinct reflectors, and the feasibility of repeated transmissions with correlation-based averaging, which together make the proposed method efficient. With the proposed method, average and local sound speed estimations yield root-mean-square errors of less than 1 and 5 m/s, respectively, for depths between 10 and 50 m. These results are achievable using an 8-m array with 20 receivers and 20 pulse repetitions with the center frequency of 300 kHz.

title: NeuroBridge-AI: A Hybrid Biophysical and Machine Learning Framework for Multi-Scale Neural Simulation
authors: Pranali Dahiwal, Govinda B. Sambare, Kuldeep Vayadande, Pavitha Nooji, Anindita Khade, Krishna Prakasha, Aditi Bhat, Dhruv Sharma, Pradnya Ingle, Jaywant Bhaskar Avhad
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Understanding how neural networks develop emergent behavior remains one of the main challenges in computational neuroscience. Models that balance the dynamics of microscopic ion-channels with macroscopic behavioral responses are important, but most strategies have lost cellular details in favor of network scale. OpenWorm c302 (302 neurons, ˜7000 synapses) and implementation of ModelDB (ModelDB 266796), a model of the human hippocampal circuit with healthy and epileptic variants, provide complementary advantages: connectome topology and complete neuronal physiology, respectively. This study offers a hybrid structure, which combines the hippocampal ModelDB-based biophysics with the network topology of c302, to allow real-time voltage propagation, synaptic co-ordination and temporal synchronization via Python-based cross-framework communication. Electro-physiological properties and locomotor emergent behaviors are reproduced in the hybrid simulation. The benchmark tests indicate a great correspondence with experimental results: AVA inter-neuron peak firing rate of 34 Hz (target 32-35 Hz), VB motor neuron synchrony index of 0.87 (target ≥ 0.85) and 96% correspondence in the sequences of body-wall muscle activation. The hybrid system maintains fine-grained neuronal fidelity and has realistic coordination on a network scale compared to stand-alone simulations of c302 or ModelDB. The condition of adaptation modulation of the synaptic weights and ion-channel conductance produces stable and non-saturating oscillatory dynamics. The hybrid system establishes a two way communication between the hippocampal and C. elegans neural models to study how biophysical and whole-network dynamics can interact to produce emergent behavior. The model combines a comprehensive model of neuronal dynamics with fully connected brain regions, which has led to research on how localized neurons processes can lead to complex organisms.

title: AlGaN-Based UV LEDs with High Wall-Plug Efficiency
authors: Wenting Gong, Michael Wang, Alejandro Quevedo, Jonathan Bigelow, Yifan Yao, Michael Iza, Abdullah Almogbel, James S. Speck, Steven P. Denbaars, Shuji Nakamura
publish: IEEE Electron Device Letters
abstract: AlGaN-based ultraviolet light-emitting diodes (UV LEDs) are considered promising solid-state UV light sources; however, their low wall-plug efficiency (WPE) has remained a major barrier to practical implementation. In this work, we designed the epitaxial structure and growth conditions for MOCVD-grown AlGaN-based UV LEDs to achieve coherent growth of the AlGaN layers and the formation of low-areal-density p-GaN islands. We further developed a processing approach to address the limited light extraction associated with highly efficient AlGaN-based UV LED epitaxial structures. A mirror p-contact and an ohmic-like n-contact were co-optimized to achieve high reflectance and reliable electrical contacts in UV LEDs with low p-GaN areal coverage, and a flip-chip geometry was adopted to further improve light extraction. High WPE of the UV LEDs was demonstrated under continuous-wave (CW) current injection. A peak WPE of 19.0% at 0.09 A/cm² and a WPE of 7.4% at 10 A/cm² were achieved for UV LED emitting at 298 nm.

title: Fully Metallic Millimeter-Wave Mikaelian Lens Multibeam Antenna
authors: Yunda Song, Hongda Lu, Zeqi Miao, Yong Liu, Meng Cao, Steven Gao
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: This paper presents a compact fully metallic Mikaelian lens antenna with enhanced multibeam capabilities at 5G-N257 band for the first time. The lens is realized based on a parallel-plate waveguide (PPW) loaded with periodic metallic nails. The gradient equivalent refractive indices are relatively low with a profile from 1.0 to 1.6, realized by easy-to-process nails at millimeter-wave (mmW). An antireflection matching layer (ARML) with structure similar to the lens is added to the output edge of the lens, reducing the reflection caused by the steeply changing refractive index. In addition, a flare and several choke grooves are also employed along with the lens to further improve the radiation performance. Width-reduced asymmetric double-ridge waveguides are designed as multibeam feeders, supporting a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\pm 30^{\circ }$</tex-math></inline-formula> beam scanning coverage with seven beams, overlap levels better than -4 dB and scan loss better than 2.4 dB. This mmW fully metallic Mikaelian lens multibeam antenna is fabricated with CNC machining and experimentally verified, providing a potential option for wireless systems requiring multiple channels.

title: A Semi-Synchronous Federated Learning Scheme for Internet of Vehicles
authors: Lin Wei, Dan Peng, Lei Shi, Huijuan Lian
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: Federated Learning demonstrates significant potential in Internet of Vehicles for facilitating collaborative task execution among vehicles while preserving data privacy. However, the direct application of conventional Federated Learning systems to Internet of Vehicles scenarios faces critical challenges, including inherent Federated Learning security risks and Internet of Vehicles environmental heterogeneity, which lead to inefficiencies in aggregation. To address these challenges, our work proposes a Semi-Synchronous Federated Learning (SSFL) scheme. To navigate the complexities of heterogeneous vehicular environments, SSFL implements a multifaceted defense and optimization architecture. The scheme adaptively manages the server’s response cycle through a dynamic waiting technique, effectively neutralizing the straggler effect. It employs a malicious gradient exclusion algorithm to preserve model integrity. Consequently, this approach ensures efficient convergence and robust training performance by addressing performance bottlenecks and security threats in a unified manner.

title: Validation-Driven Adaptive Augmentation with Minimal Overhead for Image Classification
authors: Ye Zhu, Yifei You
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: Data augmentation plays a crucial role in improving the generalization capability of deep convolutional neural networks. However, conventional augmentation strategies typically rely on manually designed and fixed transformation pipelines that remain unchanged throughout training. Such static policies cannot adapt to the evolving learning dynamics of neural networks. In this paper, we propose a validation-driven adaptive augmentation framework with minimal computational overhead for image classification. The proposed method maintains a pool of candidate augmentation policies and dynamically adjusts their sampling probabilities using validation feedback during training. After each epoch, validation accuracy is used to update the policy distribution through a lightweight normalization mechanism, enabling the model to progressively emphasize more effective augmentation strategies without reinforcement learning or expensive search procedures. Experiments on CIFAR-10 with ResNet-18 demonstrate that the proposed method consistently outperforms fixed augmentation baselines under both limited and extended training budgets, achieving 84.45% accuracy at 30 epochs and 87.35% at 100 epochs.

title: Image Colorization and CCTV Image Restoration Using Deep Learning
authors: Shreshtha Gupta, Shruti Kirti Singh, Yatharth Bhushan Tripathi, Sahil Chaudhary, Kalpna Sagar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Systems in Engineering, Secured Systems and Cybersecurity (ICISESSC)
abstract: The challenge of predicting accurate color information from grayscale images, where multiple correct color interpretations can relate to similar brightness patterns, is called picture colorization. Colorization systems have become much more automated and realistic because of recent improvements in deep learning, especially convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and generative adversarial networks (GANs). A deep learning framework is proposed for the simultaneous colorization and restoration of CCTV images in this paper. The technology tackles common issues in surveillance, including noise, blur, compression artifacts, low light, and grayscale limitations. A U-Net-based neural network is trained using images from the intentionally degraded COCO dataset to learn both restoration and color prediction in the CIELAB color space. This approach separates color and brightness learning for improved stability. The system combines reconstruction, perceptual, and adversarial losses to strike a balance between color constancy, visual realism, and structural fidelity. Surveillance-style photography is evaluated both quantitatively and qualitatively using PSNR, SSIM, and perceptual similarity criteria. Real-time inference capabilities for important surveillance applications can be demonstrated with a Flask-based deployment pipeline. By keeping structural integrity and improving the clarity of damaged footage, the framework aids forensic investigation. The discussion points out transparency, the preservation of original evidence, and the risks of AI-generated visual content when considering ethical and legal issues. The suggested method offers a practical balance between restoration quality and the ethical use of AI in forensics and security.

title: A Wideband Dual-Mode High-Efficiency MMIC Power Amplifier Based on Outphasing Architecture
authors: Letian Guo, Shunli Ma, Xincheng Shen, Weiwei Wang, Jiayu Huang, Jialin Cai, Giovanni Crupi, Shichang Chen, Junyan Ren
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: A dual-mode power amplifier (PA) with controllable output states is presented. Leveraging the advantages of the outphasing architecture, the proposed PA can achieve high efficiency at two different power modes (saturation and backed-off). To solve the bandwidth limitation problem caused by the 1/4 and 1/2 wavelength transmission lines in traditional outphasing amplifiers, ±ϕ equivalent transmission line with low phase dispersion effect is employed. The low phase dispersion characteristic of this ±ϕ equivalent transmission line is the core mechanism for bandwidth extension, which significantly reduces phase variation across the frequency band compared to traditional λ/4/λ/2 lines. Moreover, to reduce the mode switching complexity and ensure efficiency, a digitally controlled phase shifter is introduced into the signal conditioning circuit of a conventional outphasing PA. This DCPS-based single-input control scheme replaces the complex dual-input phase synchronization module of traditional outphasing PAs, simplifying system integration while enabling flexible dual-mode switching. In virtue of this design strategy, the output states of the amplifier can be artificially controlled with different back-off range. For demonstration, a 4.8 GHz monolithic microwave integrated circuit (MMIC) prototype is designed using a 0.25 μm gallium arsenide (GaAs) process. Measurement results indicate that the prototype achieves a drain efficiency higher than 47% in saturated mode across the frequency range of 3.8-5.8 GHz. Additionally, a 6 dB back-off efficiency exceeding 26.5% is obtained within the frequency range of 4.2-5.8 GHz.

title: Google's Training Supercomputers from TPUv2 to Ironwood: Architectural Stability, Scale, Resilience, Power Efficiency, and Sustainability Across Five Generations
authors: Norman P. Jouppi, Sridhar Lakshmanamurthy, Cliff Young, David Patterson
publish: IEEE Micro
abstract: This paper summarizes five generations of Google's TPUs, from TPUv2 to Ironwood, highlighting their evolution as scalable, resilient, power-efficient, sustainable supercomputers for AI training. It details the TPU’s stable architecture, which has surprisingly easily accommodated the rapidly changing deep neural network workloads, such as the rise of Transformers. Key advancements over eight years include 10x increase in HBM capacity and bandwidth per node, a 100x increase in peak node performance, and a 3600x increase in supercomputer performance. The paper also discusses the role of optical circuit switches, built-in self test, and hardware replay in enhancing resilience and how TPU's environmental impact is reduced with substantial improvements in performance per Watt and in carbon emissions per floating point operation. It concludes by identifying six features that may well characterize successful training accelerators of this decade.

title: Marine Saliency Segmenter: Object-Focused Conditional Diffusion with Region-Level Semantic Knowledge Distillation
authors: Laibin Chang, Yunke Wang, Jiaxing Huang, Longxiang Deng, Bo Du, Chang Xu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing
abstract: Marine Saliency Segmentation (MSS) plays a pivotal role in a wide range of vision-based marine exploration tasks. However, existing techniques often face the dilemma of imprecise boundaries due to the interference-rich nature of underwater environments, where suspended particles, low contrast, and color distortion hinder accurate segmentation. Although diffusion models have shown impressive performance in visual tasks, their potential to incorporate contextual semantics for enhancing feature learning of region-level salient objects remains under-explored, thereby hindering segmentation outcomes. Building on this insight, we propose DiffMSS, a novel marine saliency segmenter based on the diffusion model, which utilizes semantic knowledge distillation to guide the detection of marine salient objects. Specifically, we design the Word-level Semantic Saliency Extraction module that identifies salient terms at the word level from the captions by computing region-word similarity. These high-level semantic features are distilled into the Conditional Feature Learning Network to generate accurate and semantically informed diffusion conditions. The Object-Focused Conditional Diffusion module then leverages these conditions to iteratively generate fine-grained segmentation masks of marine instances, while a Consensus Deterministic Sampling scheme is further employed to suppress overconfident mis-segmentations and enhance structural fidelity. Extensive experiments demonstrate the superior performance of DiffMSS over state-of-the-art methods in both quantitative and qualitative evaluations. Our code and pre-trained models will be released on GitHub.

title: Multi-Dimensional Attention Autocorrelation-Based Small Sea-Surface Target Detection
authors: Yuhang Zhu, Lu Shen, Hongtao Su, Ze Li, Ruixing Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: The detection of small sea-surface targets remains challenging due to their low signal-to-clutter-plus-noise ratios (SCNR), and low Doppler frequencies. Conventional adaptive detectors fail to accurately model sea clutter, resulting in high false alarm rates and reduced detection probability. Feature-based methods avoid explicit clutter modeling but struggle to extract sufficiently discriminative features and generally require long observation durations. Although deep learning (DL) methods offer a promising alternative, most existing DL detectors rely on single-range-cell feature extraction, limiting their ability to distinguish weak targets from complex clutter backgrounds. In practice, sea clutter across adjacent range cells is typically independently and identically distributed (i.i.d.). Consequently, the autocorrelation features of a target-containing cell under test will exhibit distinct discrepancies compared with those of reference cells. Accordingly, we propose a detection method that leverages both the temporal correlation of the echo signal within each range cell and the similarity of their autocorrelation information between the cell under test and reference cells. While standard Transformers are adept at sequence processing, their inherent architecture cannot explicitly model such intra-cell autocorrelation. To realize the proposed mechanism, we introduce a neural network framework that employs a Multi-Dimensional Attention Autocorrelation (MDAttAC) module for explicit feature extraction. Subsequently, a standard Transformer is cascaded to evaluate the spatial similarity of these robust features across range cells. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method significantly enhances detection performance, reduces reliance on long observation durations, and exhibits superior generalization capability.

title: Fuzzy Implicative Rules: A Unified Approach
authors: Raquel Fernandez-Peralta
publish: IEEE Transactions on Fuzzy Systems
abstract: Rule mining algorithms are one of the fundamental techniques in data mining for discovering significant patterns in terms of linguistic rules expressed in natural language. In this paper, we revisit the concept of fuzzy implicative rules to provide a solid theoretical framework for any fuzzy rule mining algorithm interested in capturing patterns in terms of logical conditionals rather than the co-occurrence of antecedent and consequent. In particular, we study which properties the fuzzy operators should satisfy to ensure a coherent behavior of different quality measures. As a consequence of this study, we introduce a new property of fuzzy implications related to a monotone behavior of the generalized modus ponens for which we provide several valid solutions. Also, we prove that our modeling generalizes other approaches if an adequate choice of the fuzzy implication is made, so it can be seen as an unifying framework. We test the applicability and relevance of our framework on different real datasets and with different fuzzy operators.

title: Hyperspectral Remote Sensing Image Synthesis via Superpixel and Energy-Guided Sampling-based Diffusion Model
authors: Shaokai Weng, Hao Wu, Guowu Yuan, Shuanglin Zu
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Hyperspectral remote sensing images (HSIs) contain rich spectral information and have been widely used in agricultural monitoring, mineral analysis, and water monitoring. However, hyperspectral data acquisition is expensive and operationally complex, which limits its broader adoption. Hyperspectral image synthesis aims to generate HSIs conditioned on low-cost, high-resolution RGB images, thereby lowering the barrier to acquiring hyperspectral data. Existing methods typically aggregate features on regular grids using convolutions or local attention, but they lack explicit modeling of the regional-consistency prior of HSIs (pixels within the same object region usually share similar spectral shapes), which often causes spectral leakage near object boundaries and leads to texture/detail loss. Meanwhile, the intensity and signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) of HSIs are unevenly distributed across spatial regions and spectral bands, making low-illumination areas more susceptible to noise and reconstruction bias. To address these issues, we propose a superpixel-based two-stage latent diffusion model. We redesign the Vector Quantized Generative Adversarial Network (VQGAN) encoder with a spectral–spatial dual-branch architecture and introduce superpixel partitioning for region-level consistency modeling, producing lower-noise and more boundary-sensitive latent representations to enhance diffusion-based reconstruction. In addition, we construct an energy metric based on squared pixel intensity and propose an energy-adaptive dynamic guidance strategy that adaptively reweights denoising updates during sampling, encouraging the model to focus on low-energy (low-SNR, noise-sensitive) regions and thereby improving reconstruction quality and detail fidelity. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves strong performance on hyperspectral image synthesis.

title: Design and Realization of GaN-Based Trench-Gate MOSFETs Based on Graded Al-Composition AlGaN Channel Layer
authors: Yan Guo, Zexiang Liu, Guokai Wang, Hui Guo, Yugang Zhou, Dunjun Chen, Rong Zhang, Youdou Zheng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Electron Devices
abstract: In this work, a polarization-doped trench-gate MOSFET (PD-TMOSFET) structure suitable for monolithic integrated driving of microlight-emitting diodes (Micro-LEDs) is proposed. By adopting graded Al-composition AlGaN as the channel layer, the PD-TMOSFET avoids a series of issues inherent to conventional Mg-doped MOSFETs. Comparative TCAD simulations of Ga- and N-face configurations revealed superior electrical characteristics in Ga-face devices. Guided by these simulations, the PD-TMOSFET was fabricated, achieving a specific <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">on</small>-resistance of 10.74 m<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\Omega \cdot \text {cm}^{{2}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>, a threshold voltage of 1.6 V (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\text {V}_{\text {DS}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> = 1 V and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\text {I}_{\text {DS}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> = <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$1~\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>A), an <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${I}_{\mathrm {ON}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>/<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${I}_{\mathrm {OFF}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> ratio of approximately <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${1}\times {10}^{{9}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>, and a peak channel mobility of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$5.22~\text {cm}^{{2}}\text {/}\text {(}\text {V}\cdot \text {s}\text {)}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>. The devices exhibit an ultrafast transient response even at 100 kHz, significantly outperforming mainstream thin-film transistor (TFT) drivers for Micro-LED displays. Therefore, the PD-TMOSFET technology provides a promising pathway for high-performance Micro-LED display drivers and full-system, monolithic integration.

title: TSC-DETR: DETR with Transformer-Semantics to CNN Guidance for Small Object Detection
authors: Zheng Yu, Li Guo, Hongyuan Huo, Qing Xu, Fang Ren, Qifan Cui
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Object detection models based on the Detection Transformer (DETR) architecture suffer from a performance bottleneck due to its attention mechanism’s bias toward high-level semantic information, resulting in insufficient low-level detail feature extraction. Particularly in small object detection, existing DETR models suffer from significant deficiencies in detail representation due to the limited number of pixels in the target region. To address this issue, we propose the Transformer-Semantics to CNN Guidance DEtection TRansformer (TSC-DETR), which leverages high-level semantic guidance and a CNN to enhance low-level edge feature extraction for small object detection. First, we introduce a method to enhance the encoder’s dynamic multi-scale perception capabilities to compensate for the linear attention mechanism’s limitations in describing edge detail. Secondly, we utilize a high-level semantic prior prediction guidance map generated by the Transformer encoder to guide the convolutional network to focus more on positive sample regions. Also, to address the problem of small objects being missed due to the ”flooding effect” of high-level semantics in negative sample regions, we design a prediction guidance map specifically for negative sample regions to improve the model’s ability to recognize small objects. Furthermore, we constructed a multi-scale, multi-granularity detail perception module. This module relies on high-level semantics to focus on positive and negative regions, fusing multi-level information to enhance the expressive power of low-level features in depicting the boundaries and textures of small objects. TSC-DETR outperforms previous CNN-based and DETR-based methods, achieving an AP of 31.5% on AI-TOD and 36.7% on Visdrone2019.The project page:https://github.com/AuroraLove/tsc-detr.

title: Micro Multi-agent Reinforcement Learning Systems for Tumor Therapy: A Consensus Control Approach
authors: Neshat Elhami Fard, Behnaz Merikhi, Rastko R. Selmic, Rhonda McEwen
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Cancer remains one of the leading causes of mortality worldwide, and conventional treatment methods such as surgery, chemotherapy, and radiation therapy are often associated with significant side effects and limitations in accessibility. Non-invasive targeted tumor therapy using micro-scale agents has emerged as a promising alternative, yet precisely coordinating multiple micro-agents within complex biological environments while ensuring safety and convergence remains a significant challenge. In this paper, we present a consensus control framework for micro multi-agent reinforcement learning (MMARL) systems equipped with a collision avoidance system (CAS) for safely and precisely targeting cancerous tumors in vascular environments. Our framework utilizes distributed micro-agents using Q-learning and position consensus to autonomously navigate toward tumor sites while avoiding collisions and respecting vascular boundaries.We present standard and cooperative position consensus approaches and mathematically investigate their convergence, robustness under perturbations, and regret bounds. Simulations demonstrate that the cooperative consensus approach significantly improves convergence speed, targeting accuracy, and stability, suggesting an advantageous non-invasive option for tumor treatment.

title: Integrated Transportation–Distribution Network Planning for Electric Vehicle Fast Charging Stations With Queueing Based Sizing and Renewable Support
authors: Digambar Singh Govind, Alexander Aguila Téllez, Manoj Fozdar, Narayanan Krishnan
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: The increasing penetration of electric vehicles (EVs) and the deployment of fast charging stations (FCSs) create strong interactions between transportation networks and power distribution systems, potentially leading to traffic congestion, voltage degradation, and increased feeder losses. This paper presents an integrated transportation–power network framework to assess the operational impacts of EV fast charging and to support optimal planning of FCSs, solar photovoltaic (SPV) systems, and battery energy storage systems (BESSs). EV mobility is modelled using the Sioux Falls transportation network, where distance-weighted shortest routes are identified for all origin–destination pairs using the Floyd–Warshall algorithm. Traffic flow and normalized temporal arrival patterns are used to derive station-specific EV arrival rates. The resulting charging demand is mapped to the IEEE 69-bus radial distribution system through coupled nodes on the transportation model. The queueing-theory formulation is applied in the sizing of charging ports at each FCS, ensuring queue stability and a maximum allowable waiting time. EV penetration scenarios are investigated to evaluate the scalability of the fast-charging station and the distribution system. A time-coupled load flow analysis is conducted to examine voltage profiles, the voltage deviation index, and active power losses. Results show that uncoordinated FCS integration causes noticeable voltage drops and increased losses, particularly at higher EVs penetration levels. Coordinated integration and sizing of SPV and BESS at FCS locations significantly improves voltage regulation and reduces system losses while maintaining acceptable charging service performance. The proposed framework provides a practical, scalable approach for planning EV fast-charging infrastructure to support increased EV adoption.

title: Learning from hard negative samples: Multi-prototype contrastive learning for out-of-distribution detection
authors: Jinhyuk Suh, Youngbum Hur
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Out-of-distribution (OOD) detection is an essential task when we deploy the trained deep learning model with the closed-world assumption to real-world scenarios. Recently, many researchers use prototypes representing the mean of each in-distribution (ID) class as anchors in contrastive learning. This approach allows samples within a class to cluster more tightly for compact ID data embeddings while enabling dispersed embeddings for OOD data. As studies utilizing prototypes shift from single to multi-prototype approaches, some prototypes positioned farther from the class center become vulnerable to hard negative samples; these samples have similar features to the anchor prototype. To alleviate this issue, we propose ADAPT, a novel strategy to adapt for hard negative samples on multi-prototype contrastive learning. This approach dynamically adjusts the weight for negative samples, enabling the model to robustly handle hard negative samples and leading to more compact intra-class and dispersed inter-class embeddings for ID data. Moreover, to ensure training stability for initial unstable prototypes, we quantify prototype alignment level and adjust the temperature coefficient used for training accordingly. Consequently, ADAPT demonstrates state-of-the-art performance on both standard and more challenging Near-OOD detection benchmarks.

title: Design of a Low-Cost Real-Time Angle Sensor Module for Dynamic Body-Weight Support Systems Using Inertial Measurement Unit and Fusion Algorithms
authors: Hossein Yaghoobzadeh Shadmehri, Shahin Jafarabadi Ashtiani, Ehsan Rahiminejad, Afsaneh Zeinalzadeh, Reza Lotfi
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: Dynamic Body Weight-Support (DBWS) systems are used for patients with mobility impairments. These systems provide three key functions—dynamic body-weight support, fall prevention, and synchronized propulsion—all of which were designed and implemented in this study. One of the most important parameters in a DBWS system is the angle between the patient and the device, which is measured by an angle-estimation module. The problem addressed in this research is the design and construction of a low-cost angle measurement module that is minimally sensitive to vibration, maintains high precision, and has a fast response time. This module is suitable for rehabilitation applications (for example DBWS system) that require high precision and are subject to significant vibration. In this paper, by using an inertial measurement unit (IMU) sensor and a switching logic between different filters based on data fusion, low sensitivity to vibration (vibrations caused by mechanical oscillations) and high response speed are achieved. Outputs of Madgwick and Kalman filters (MF and KF) are fused to obtain the angle from the IMU. The MF is employed to detect rapid movements, and two KFs with high and low measurement noise values (HMNV-KF and LMNV-KF) are used to measure the angle. When rapid angular changes occur, the output angle is obtained from the LMNV-KF; otherwise, the HMNV-KF is used. Additionally, a low-pass filter is applied to the LMNV-KF output after fast angular changes have ended but before the HMNV-KF reaching its steady value. The results obtained from the DBWS system show that the proposed algorithm outperforms all other filters in terms of response speed and vibration sensitivity, making it highly suitable for the intended application. The rise time is 171.33 ms, and the maximum error is 0.54°, both of which are lower than those of all other tested filters.

title: MOF-Derived Ni/NiO on Cu-Foam Electrode modified for Selective and Ultrasensitive Electrochemical Creatinine Detection_supp1-3695569.docx
authors: Sushmee Badhulika
publish: N/A
abstract: Creatinine is a clinically important biomarker for renal function assessment; abnormally low levels may reflect muscle degradation, whereas elevated levels are associated with underlying kidney dysfunction. Therefore, accurate monitoring is essential for early detection and disease management. In this study, we report a novel metal-organic framework (MOF)-derived Ni/NiO heterostructure, synthesized via a solvothermal route, integrated onto a copper foam (CF) electrode, for sensing creatinine in sweat samples. The formation of Ni/NiO heterostructures is evident from the SEM characterization, providing a high density of accessible electroactive sites. FTIR spectrum gives vibrational features associated with Ni–O bonding. Optimization studies of Ni/NiO@CF electrode (7 wt.%, pH 7.2) are performed in the window 0.4-1.6 V vs Hg|HgO, and improved electron-transfer rates and electrode kinetics are obtained by EIS. The DPV analysis demonstrates detection of creatinine with a wide linear response range (0.01-100 μM) [R² = 0.99], ultrahigh sensitivity (6.2 μA μM⁻¹ cm⁻²), and an impressively low detection limit (0.306 nM), making it suitable for non-invasive trace-level analysis. The sensor exhibits excellent performance, satisfactory operational stability, and reproducibility with recovery values of 99.96%, along with outstanding selectivity against common sweat interferents such as dopamine, glucose, urea, and uric acid. Finally, the practicality of the sensor is validated by quantifying creatinine in simulated human sweat samples, establishing its potential for future wearable Point-of-Care (POC) applications in personalized health monitoring.

title: Deep Reinforcement Learning Enabled Persistent Surveillance with Energy-Aware UAV-UGV Systems for Disaster Management Applications_supp2-3696943.mp4
authors: Md Safwan Mondal
publish: N/A
abstract: Integrating Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) with Unmanned Ground Vehicles (UGVs) provides an effective solution for persistent surveillance in disaster management. UAVs excel at covering large areas rapidly, but their range is limited by battery capacity. UGVs, though slower, can carry larger batteries for extended missions. By using UGVs as mobile recharging stations, UAVs can extend mission duration through periodic refueling, leveraging the complementary strengths of both systems. To optimize this energy-aware UAV-UGV cooperative routing problem, we propose a planning framework that determines optimal routes and recharging points between a UAV and a UGV. Our solution employs a deep reinforcement learning (DRL) framework built on an encoder-decoder transformer architecture with multi-head attention mechanisms. This architecture enables the model to sequentially select actions for visiting mission points and coordinating recharging rendezvous between the UAV and UGV. The DRL model is trained to minimize the age periods (the time gap between consecutive visits) of mission points, ensuring effective surveillance. We evaluate the framework across various problem sizes and distributions, comparing its performance against heuristic methods and an existing learning-based model. Results show that our approach consistently outperforms these baselines in both solution quality and runtime. Additionally, we demonstrate the DRL policy's applicability in a real-world disaster scenario as a case study and explore its potential for online mission planning to handle dynamic changes. Adapting the DRL policy for priority-driven surveillance highlights the model's generalizability for real-time disaster response. More details are available on our website: https://sites.google.com/view/ecucps.

title: Flexible &#x03B2; - Phase PVDF-Cu-MAA Self-Powered Nanofiber Nanogenerator for Energy Harvesting Applications_supp1-3698890.pdf
authors: Satinder Kumar Sharma
publish: N/A
abstract: This work reports the efficient development of polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) nanofibers doped with copper methacrylate (Cu-MAA) to form metal-organic clusters (MOCs), demonstrating notable energy generation and harvesting capabilities, and having strong potential for flexible electronics applications. A flexible piezoelectric nanogenerator (PENG) device was fabricated using electrospun PVDF-Cu-MAA nanofibers. These nanofibers form a matrix embedded between two flexible electrodes to fabricate device structures capable of converting mechanical force into electrical energy, and thereby enabling self-powered sensing. The pristine PVDF-based PENG devices and PVDF-Cu-MAA hybrid PENG devices produced a peak-to-peak output voltage of approximately 2.14V and 6.14V under mechanical tapping, respectively. The incorporation of 5 w/v% Cu-MAA significantly improved the piezoelectric response without compromising the flexibility of the polymer mat. These enhancements are primarily attributed to the increased formation of the polar β-phase in the hybrid nanofibers. Furthermore, the PENG devices were integrated with a DHT11 sensor and EPS-32 microcontroller, enabling real-time temperature and humidity sensing. These findings demonstrate the high potential of Cu-MAA-doped PVDF hybrid nanofibers PENG device for use in self-powered energy harvesting systems and sustainable sensing applications.

title: Numerical Simulation and Optimization of a-IGZO Thin Film Transistor
authors: Abhinandan Jain, Praveen Kumar Jain
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Trends in Electronics and Informatics (ICOEI)
abstract: This work investigates multiple thin-film transistor (TFT) architectures through simulation to identify the most effective structural configuration for enhanced performance. Different staggered and coplanar arrangements—namely BGTC, BGBC, TGTC, and TGBC— were analyzed to evaluate their electrical characteristics. The comparative study determines the most suitable structure for achieving improved performance in display and sensing applications. The evaluated device parameters for the BGTC, BGBC, TGTC, and TGBC structures calculated field-effect mobility values are 20.62, 3.96, 0.99, and 20.56 cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>/V·s, respectively. Their corresponding on/off current ratios are 3.09 × 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">13</sup>, 1.14 × 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">12</sup>, 1.21 × 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">11</sup>, and 1.55 × 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">13</sup> and threshold voltage values are determined to be 2.29 V, -1.19 V, −0.22 V, and 2.28 V, while the measured subthreshold swings are 0.21, 0.14, 0.27, and 0.36 V/decade for the respective configurations. The bottom gate top contact indicated the significant results amoung all other structure due to efficient carrier injection, reduced contact resistance, strong electrostatic control, and improved interface quality.

title: Transformer-Based Deep Learning Architecture for Automated Sleep Disorder Detection and Severity Analysis
authors: Shree Smeka J, Sheeja Kumari V, Murugan A. S. S., Kadamanchi Santhosha, Santhoshkumar S. P., Jim Mathew Philip
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Emerging Trends in Advancements and Applications of Computational Intelligence Techniques (ETAACT)
abstract: Insomnia is a long-term sleeping disorder that is characterized by recurring problems with falling asleep, keeping sleep or waking up too soon causing poor functioning of the daytime activity and high risks of psychiatric and cardiovascular comorbidity. The subjective reporting of symptoms and the dynamic aspects of time as seen in polysomnography (PSG) make it difficult to achieve accurate diagnosis. The current paper will suggest a self-adaptive and explainable transformer based deep learning model of automated sleep disorder detection and severity according to the main emphasis on insomnia based on multimodal PSG data. The framework utilizes the adaptive signal preprocessing and the time-frequency representation to help in effectively describing the subtle temporal and spectral changes in EEG, ECG, respiratory signals, and the oxygen saturation of insomnia-related sleeping disruptions. The proposed hybrid deep architecture that incorporates convolutional deep architecture, residual deep architecture, and transformer-based attention is aimed to identify the long-range dependence and micro-arousal patterns across sleep stages. Moreover, performance on an uneven and unbalanced patient population is enhanced with the use of ensemble learning and imbalance-sensitive optimization mechanisms. Evaluations in experimental settings using benchmark PSG datasets show that the proposed method obtains higher accuracy and balanced sensitivity-specificity performance than the existing machine learning and deep learning strategies, and it offers clinically meaningful interpretability, thus, leading to its application as a valuable decision support approach to insomnia assessment.

title: Investigating Performance of Voting Ensemble Model on Bearing Fault Classification
authors: Ezgi Orhan, Ahmet Sinan Oktem
publish: 2026 8th International Congress on Human-Computer Interaction, Optimization and Robotic Applications (ICHORA)
abstract: Motor Current Signal Analysis (MCSA) has emerged as a cost-effective alternative to vibration-based monitoring for rolling element bearings, since it utilizes existing single-phase stator current signals without requiring additional sensors. However, extracting fault signatures from current signals remains challenging due to the dominance of the fundamental power supply frequency and the presence of high-frequency noise, which often mask weak fault characteristics. This paper uses the Paderborn University damaged bearing dataset, consisting of stator current data from healthy, real damaged inner and real damaged outer raceway bearings and also their combinations with different fault severity. Feature Reconstruction method (FR method) was applied for extracting fault signature from the current signals. The resulted signal was converted into frequency domain after extracting the envelope signal with Hilbert Transform. Random Forest (RF), Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost), Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LightGBM), were selected for their demonstrated ability to capture complex nonlinear patterns and their robustness against overfitting. Hyperparameter tuning was conducted for each model using the Optuna framework, which applies Bayesian optimization to select the best parameter settings. While deep learning approaches have shown success in extracting features from reconstructed signals, they often suffer from high computational complexity and extensive hyperparameter tuning requirements. To address these limitations, this study proposes a computationally efficient Ensemble Learning framework. Experimental results demonstrate that this lightweight architecture achieves diagnostic accuracy comparable to state-of-the-art CNN models while significantly reducing training time and computational resources, making it highly suitable for realtime industrial monitoring systems. The ensemble model utilizing soft voting achieved an overall accuracy of 0.94.

title: Inductor-Based Active Cell Balancing Strategy for Electric Vehicle Battery Management Systems
authors: Erdinç Aslan, Ozan Yazar, Serdar Coşkun, Fengqi Zhang
publish: 2026 8th International Congress on Human-Computer Interaction, Optimization and Robotic Applications (ICHORA)
abstract: The imbalance of cells in electric vehicle (EV) battery packs reduces usable capacity and shortens the useful life of the battery packs. Although existing approaches rely on constantcurrent testing and voltage measurements, both of which may be inadequate given the flat discharge profile of lithium-ion batteries. To address this gap, we study an inductor-based buck-boost active equalizer using State of Charge (SoC) as the primary control variable, which is tested under realistic and highly dynamic EV driving conditions. Rather than relying on predefined laboratory tests, real-time power demand is extracted from an ADVISORbased EV model and applied to a 5-cell, 50 Ah lithium-ion module under the Urban Dynamometer Driving Schedule (UDDS). The cell balancing scheme then utilizes a cell-to-cell energy transfer architecture managed by a rule-based algorithm with a 1 % SoC deadband to prevent unnecessary high-frequency chattering. Designed to operate safely in Continuous Conduction Mode (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$50 \mu H, 10 \text{kHz}$</tex>), the proposed setup demonstrates that Active SoC-based balancing remains stable, precise, and highly efficient even when subjected to the harsh, fluctuating acceleration and regenerative braking currents of actual urban driving.

title: Comprehensive Study of Data-Driven Approaches for Ready Biodegradability Classification
authors: Kundjanasith Thonglek, Palida Pongsanon, Autchara Pangon
publish: 2026 IEEE Conference on Technologies for Sustainability (SusTech)
abstract: Accurate prediction of ready biodegradability is essential for chemical risk assessment and sustainable chemical design, yet remains challenging due to the complex and multivariate nature of molecular properties governing biodegradation behavior. While numerous machine learning models have been applied to this task, prior studies often lack systematic comparison, rigorous validation, and interpretability, limiting their practical applicability. In this paper, we investigate machine learning models for classifying chemical compounds according to their ready biodegradability. The study is conducted using a quantitative structure-activity relationship dataset consisting of 41 molecular descriptors with experimentally validated biodegradability annotations. A wide range of classification algorithms are systematically benchmarked under a unified evaluation protocol using stratified five-fold and nested crossvalidation to avoid optimistic bias. Feature selection and stability analysis are employed to identify robust and informative descriptor subsets, followed by principled hyperparameter tuning of candidate models. The experimental results demonstrate that a tuned support vector machine with an RBF kernel trained on 20 stability-selected descriptors achieves the best overall performance, reaching a mean balanced accuracy of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$86.10 \% \pm 1.21 \%$</tex>. Comparable performance is obtained using less than half of the original descriptors, revealing substantial redundancy in the full feature set. Furthermore, explainable AI analysis based on SHapley Additive exPlanations provides quantitative insight into descriptor relevance, showing that biodegradability predictions are driven by a compact set of chemically meaningful features.

title: Energy-Efficient Optimization of FinFET Based Compute-In-Memory SRAM Arrays
authors: T Radhika, Ranjini Priya J, Mathan N, T Ravi
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Communication and Signal Processing (ICCSP)
abstract: The increasing disparity between processor performance and memory access efficiency has intensified interest in memory-centric computing architectures. Static random-access memory (SRAM), extensively used in caches and on-chip buffers, faces growing challenges related to power consumption, access delay, and stability with technology scaling. SRAM-based compute-in-memory (CIM) architectures have gained attention by reducing data movement and improving energy efficiency by computation within memory arrays, However, a consistent evaluation of CIM-enabled SRAM across different technology nodes remains limited. This paper presents an HSPICE-based analysis of conventional 6T SRAM and CIM-enabled 6T SRAM architectures implemented using 130 nm and 32 nm MOSFET technologies and 7 nm FinFET technology. Performance metrics, including read/write delay, static noise margin (SNM), and power-delay product, are evaluated using transient simulations and butterfly curve analysis. The analysis covers both bit-cell-level and arraylevel implementations, incorporating peripheral circuits such as sense amplifiers and row decoders, are considered. The study provide insights into technology-dependent tradeoffs and highlight the impact of CIM integration on SRAM performance and energy efficiency.

title: AI-Driven Brain Signal Analysis for Early Detection and Diagnosis of Neurological Disorders
authors: Ediz Arkin Kobak, Ozgur Imer, Ali Berat Akoglu, Yagmur Dagdemir, Bahar Karadas, Sude Rudar, Kubranur Danisan, Serhat Uzunbayir
publish: 2026 8th International Congress on Human-Computer Interaction, Optimization and Robotic Applications (ICHORA)
abstract: Mild Cognitive Impairment (MCI) represents a critical transitional state between normal cognitive aging and dementia, particularly Alzheimer's disease. Early detection and accurate prognosis of MCI are essential for timely therapeutic intervention. This study investigates the application of machine learning and AI-driven approaches for analyzing Electroencephalogram (EEG) signals to address three fundamental research questions: (1) whether AI models show potential to predict disease prognosis (early detection), (2) whether they can accurately diagnose current disease status, and (3) whether brain factors influencing disease progression can be identified. Our comprehensive methodology encompasses data integration of behavioral and EEG recordings, extensive exploratory data analysis including topographic mapping and cluster-based statistical tests, rigorous preprocessing with KNN imputation and StandardScaler normalization, and systematic model benchmarking. We evaluated multiple classifiers including Logistic Regression, Support Vector Machines, Random Forest, K-Nearest Neighbors, Naive Bayes, and XGBoost. For baseline diagnosis (HC vs. MCI), XGBoost achieved the highest accuracy of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$87.92 \% \pm 6.71 \%$</tex>. For longitudinal prognosis prediction, KNN demonstrated superior performance at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{6 1. 5 1 \%} \pm \mathbf{1 3. 8 6 \%}$</tex> using Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation. Explainable AI techniques, including feature importance analysis and t-SNE visualization, revealed specific EEG features in parietal and central regions as significant biomarkers. This work contributes to the development of noninvasive, objective, and cost-effective tools for early neurological diagnosis and prognosis.

title: XpressWeed: Meta-Inspired Few-Shot Adaptation for Plant Weed Segmentation Using Texture Priors
authors: Kiran K. Kethineni, Rishi Raj Kanukuntla, Saraju P. Mohanty, Elias Kougianos
publish: 2026 IEEE Conference on Technologies for Sustainability (SusTech)
abstract: Weeds remain a persistent challenge in agriculture, competing with primary crops for resources. Recent advances in artificial intelligence have applied convolutional neural networks (CNNs) for weed detection and segmentation. However, building a single large-scale model capable of handling the vast variability in weed species is computationally infeasible and impractical. Furthermore, conventional object-centric detection and segmentation models struggle in real-world scenarios where leaves overlap, twist and exhibit considerable variations in shape and hue under changing illumination, causing traditional fewshot learning strategies to fail. To address these challenges, we introduce XpressWeed, a novel texture-prior driven segmentation framework that models plant leaves as textures, leveraging the fact that each species exhibits distinctive texture patterns which remain relatively invariant to shape deformations and occlusions. By pretraining a CNN segmentation model on a diverse collection of leaf textures, the framework learns discriminative representations that remain robust to lighting variations and morphological distortions. Subsequently, a Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) strategy is employed to adapt the pre-trained model to novel weed species with only a few labeled samples, thereby retaining its discriminative capacity while enabling rapid transfer to unseen classes. The Fully Convolutional Network (FCN) model, pre-trained on 1 2 diverse plant textures, was successfully adapted to 4 previously unseen species, achieving 85% accuracy under a 12 -shot learning setup using only 80 images while traditional MAML achieved 48% accuracy.

title: Wide Band and High Gain 1x2 Decagon Array Patch Antenna for 5GHz Applications
authors: N. Nandini, Gnanou Florence Sudha
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Trends in Electronics and Informatics (ICOEI)
abstract: Microstrip antennas have earned significant importance in wireless systems because of their compact structure, low weight. This study shows the design and performance analysis of a Single Decagon Antenna (SDA) and a 1×2 Decagon Array Antenna (DAA) working at 5 GHz. At first, the SDA geometry structure was implemented with 25.5x27.5x1.6mm size on FR4 substrate and simulation resulted in return loss of-22db, VSWR of 1.14 and gain of 2.7db. To enhance the performance of the antenna, the array geometry structure was implemented, with size of 31.1x62.1x1.6 mm, using the same substrate. Key performance parameters such as return loss, Voltage Standing Wave Ratio (VSWR), impedance, gain, 2D radiation pattern and current density were analyzed using Ansys HFSS software. The proposed array demonstrates a return loss of –42.33 dB, VSWR of 1.17, and a gain of 6.4 dB at 5 GHz, which is an improvement over existing designs confirming its suitability for wireless communication applications.

title: Prediction of Power Quality Issues by Using Machine Learning
authors: P. Sesi Kiran, J. Surya Kumari, D. Lenine, M. Naveena, V. Naveenkumar, N. Bharath
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Communication and Signal Processing (ICCSP)
abstract: Power quality problems such as voltage sag, swell, harmonics, and interruptions are known for degrading power system reliability and efficiency, which is further increased in modern power systems due to power quality events. Nonlinear loads and renewable energy integration are on an aggressive growth trajectory, which has further increased power quality problems. This paper aims to provide an approach for power quality problems using machine learning technology. Features are extracted from signals according to the required power system voltage and other relevant data, and then data processing tasks are done, ensuring the deployment of overall machine learning technology for power systems. The performance of the proposed technique is evaluated using various conventional parameters where the predicted results are compared with the actual system. The proposed technique would help power system operators improve the reliability and efficiency of power systems.

title: Hybrid Blockchain Model for Privacy Preserving Supply Chain Data Sharing
authors: Keerthana M K, Dayana D S
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Trends in Electronics and Informatics (ICOEI)
abstract: The project is a Java-based Supply Chain Management System presented to enhance the transparency, traceability and accountability through five modules, interconnected together; Maker, Processor, Distributor, Customer and Admin. This system allows tracking the product flow end-to-end, linking the QR code-based tracking, which means that every product has a unique and verifiable digital identity. Outputs (maker) document the origin and manufacture of products that are safely stored and transferred using processing and distribution channels. Product information regarding quality verifications, package and shipping are updated by processors and distributors and are maintained by the whole lifecycle. There is also an option to scan QR codes that will allow customes to verify products, track their history, purchase goods, and check their previous orders. The Admin supervises activities, approvals, and transactions of the systems. The system increases credibility, streamlining, and offers a secure and efficient platform to supply chains of the modern world.

title: A Lightweight Phase-Aware U-Net for Single-Channel Speech Enhancement in Disaster Communication Systems
authors: Sivasakthiselvan S, Siddharth Hariharan, Dhruv Arjun, Baranidharan B
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Communication and Signal Processing (ICCSP)
abstract: This research introduces a phase-aware deep learning approach for single-channel speech denoising that concurrently estimates magnitude and phase information in the time-frequency domain. A lightweight convolutional U-Net predicts a soft spectral mask and a residual phase correction from the short-time Fourier transform (STFT) of speech that contains noise in it. We test the system on paired clean and noisy speech data at different signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) levels, from 0 to 40 dB. PESQ, STOI, SNR, and SI-SDR are some of the objective and perceptual measures used to measure performance. The experimental results reveal that the suggested methodology works well for both medium- and high-SNR circumstances, with high speech intelligibility and perceptual quality. This shows that the system is ready for practical and real-time use.

title: Artificial Language Intelligence for Speech Assistance Using Phoneme Analysis
authors: G Ramesh, S Renusri, P Kabitha, Asha Judi, Manju C Nair
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Communication and Signal Processing (ICCSP)
abstract: Building automatic spoken English training systems requires accurate pronunciation error detection; however, phoneme level evaluation approaches are computationally expensive and unsuitable for real time feedback due to reliance on large ASR models or complex alignment pipelines. To address this, we propose a two stage hybrid pronunciation assessment framework that combines fast acoustic alignment with fine grained phoneme level analysis. The system first verifies sentence level correctness using a lightweight Whisper model [1], followed by raw phoneme extraction using a Wav2Vec2 based phoneme recognizer. Expected phoneme sequences are generated using eSpeak [2], and a greedy alignment strategy computes word level phoneme similarity scores by comparing predicted and reference sequences. While this approach provides accurate phoneme level feedback, processing the complete audio stream incurs significant computational cost. To improve efficiency, the framework introduces an acoustic localization stage based on mel spectrogram Dynamic Time Warping alignment between the learner's speech and a reference text to speech signal. Pronunciation deviation regions are identified using difference energy peaks, and the corresponding temporal boundaries are mapped back to the original audio through the DTW warping path. Only these localized error segments, typically covering <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 - 1 5 \%}$</tex> of the utterance, are forwarded for detailed phoneme analysis. This selective processing strategy results in an approximate FLOPs reduction of 67.78%, significantly improving computational efficiency without compromising assessment accuracy. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed system reliably detects mispronunciations with fine grained temporal precision and remains robust under variations in speaking rate, making it well suited for real time pronunciation training applications.

title: Methods for Driver Fatigue Detection Using Computer Vision and Machine Learning
authors: Bauyrzhan Smakanov, Indira Uvaliyeva
publish: 2026 8th International Congress on Human-Computer Interaction, Optimization and Robotic Applications (ICHORA)
abstract: This paper presents methods for developing an intelligent driver fatigue monitoring system using machine learning techniques. Driver fatigue is a major cause of traffic accidents, particularly during long routes and night shifts. The proposed model uses the eye aspect ratio and a support vector machine classifier to detect blinks and other fatigue indicators in real time. Special attention is given to the model's robustness under varying lighting conditions and head orientations, improving reliability in challenging operational environments. The system has been tested on video sequences with different lighting and head poses, demonstrating high accuracy and minimal false detections. The results indicate that the proposed approach is suitable for integration into intelligent transportation systems, providing a practical solution for continuous driver monitoring. The key advantages of the system are its real-time performance and resilience to changing conditions, which significantly enhance the precision and dependability of fatigue detection.

title: Application of a Systematic MBSE-Based Procedure to the Mechatronic Design of an Intralogistics Autonomous Self-Balancing Vehicle
authors: Paolo Righettini, Roberto Strada, Filippo Cortinovis, Jasmine Santinelli, Andrea Martinenghi, Marco Rebellin, Vittorio Lorenzi
publish: 2026 8th International Congress on Human-Computer Interaction, Optimization and Robotic Applications (ICHORA)
abstract: In the context of Industry 4.0 and 5.0, autonomous mobile robotics has become a cornerstone for enhancing efficiency in intralogistics. Among different configurations, Coaxial Two-Wheeled Self-Balancing Vehicles (CTWSBVs) offer superior agility and compactness compared to traditional four-wheeled platforms, making them ideal for navigating narrow warehouse environments. However, the systematic design of such complex, underactuated, and multi-domain systems is often overlooked in the literature, which tends to focus predominantly on control algorithms rather than comprehensive mechatronic development. This paper addresses this gap by applying a general systematic design procedure based on Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE), previously developed by the authors, to the specific case of an autonomous self-balancing vehicle for industrial logistics. By leveraging the ARCADIA method, the vehicle is described through an interconnected architecture of components and interfaces, facilitating the identification of critical physical and logical flows. This systematic approach guides the sizing and co-design of the mechanical, electrical, and control subsystems. To demonstrate the robustness and generality of the procedure, the resulting design is validated through a co-simulation in the MSC Adams-Matlab environment. The simulation results confirm the vehicle's dynamic stability and performance during typical operational cycles. The work proves that the proposed MBSEbased procedure is a versatile and reliable strategy for the development of different classes of autonomous self-balancing vehicles.

title: Intelligent Parking Space Allocation Using Integrated GNN, LSTM, and Deep RL: A Comprehensive Approach to Smart City Infrastructure
authors: N. Abirami, S. Dhamotharan, Anitha Rajakumari P
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Communication and Signal Processing (ICCSP)
abstract: Parking in urban areas is one of the major concerns, leading to wastage of time, fuel, and pollution. The paper proposes an intelligent parking system that can automatically assign parking spaces to vehicles using artificial intelligence. The proposed system in this paper uses three types of AI models, namely Graph Neural Networks (GNN) to study the graph representation of the parking space, Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) to study when the parking spaces are full and empty, and Deep Q-Network (DQN or RL) to take smart decisions on the assignment of the parking space to vehicles. The proposed system analyzes the graph of the parking space, approximates the time variability of occupancy of the parking space, and trains it to become efficient in making decisions on parking space allocation. The performance of the proposed system is analyzed using practical data of parking space in a 100-space parking space with sensors. An evaluation of the proposed system shows high allocation success (100% in experimental conditions) in parking space allocation, reduces average walking distance from entry to parking to only 0.30 units, and shows consistent performance in parking place allocation with an average reward of 20.87 in 500 training experiences. This proposed system maintains 29.33% occupancy while supporting real-time vehicle detection and automated payment system processing. This proposed system proves its viability in practical implementation with really fast response times and has the potential to be scaled up in parking lots in cities.

title: Deep Learning based Few-Shot Industrial Defect Detection using Diffusion Priors in X-Ray Imagery for Enhanced Generalization
authors: P M Siva Raja, Ravi Teja Bhima, G.Nageswara Rao, Ramakrishna Thirupathi, R. Naveenkumar, Dr.S.Karthigaiveni
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Trends in Electronics and Informatics (ICOEI)
abstract: Industrial defect detection in radiographic imagery is critical for quality assurance in manufacturing, aerospace, and infrastructure inspection. Nevertheless, deep learning methods often need much annotated data, which is not feasible with rare or novel defects. In order to do this, we will suggest a new few-shot learning model that uses diffusion priors to improve generalization in industrial X-ray imaging. Our model combines a Swin Transformer backbone with a denoising diffusion probabilistic model (DDPM) to learn to learn the generative defect priors based on limited support examples. Such priors are then combined with an augmented prototypical network classifier with cross-attention, which then allows the model to predict and localize unknown defect types even in low-data conditions. We test our model on benchmark datasets including GDXray and DAGM and also on cross-domain unseen datasets including WeldX-M and AeroNDT. In the 5-way 1-shot scenario, our model attains the highest accuracy of 96.88, which is substantially higher than the usual few-shot baselines such as MatchingNet, ProtoNet and RelationNet. The importance of the conditional diffusion prior in generalization improvement is confirmed in ablation studies. Moreover, the model shows consistent behavior under higher number of shots with smaller gains after 5 shots, which is effective evidence of the efficiency of the model in low-data regimes.

title: From Post-Hoc Regulation to Runtime Governance: Ethics and Explainability in AI-Driven Education
authors: Mirka Saarela, Prashanth Shenoy
publish: 2026 8th International Congress on Human-Computer Interaction, Optimization and Robotic Applications (ICHORA)
abstract: The rapid integration of artificial intelligence (AI) into education is reshaping teaching and learning through personalized feedback, automated assessment, and predictive decision support. At the same time, AI-driven educational systems raise significant ethical and governance challenges, including concerns related to transparency, explainability, fairness, and the protection of students' rights.
