This paper compares two complementary approaches to governing AI in educational contexts: external regulatory frameworks, exemplified by the European Union's AI Act and national AI governance guidelines, and design-embedded ethical frameworks, exemplified by Anthropic's Claude's Constitution. Adopting a temporal perspective on AI governance, the paper analyzes how regulatory approaches primarily operationalize explainability and accountability through post-hoc documentation, auditability, and compliance mechanisms, whereas design-embedded ethical frameworks integrate normative constraints (such as transparency, forthrightness, and the avoidance of hidden agendas) directly into system behavior at runtime. Drawing on an analysis of Claude's constitutional principles, the paper examines how explainability can be enacted as an interaction-level property rather than a retrospective obligation. By situating these approaches within broader debates on ethical AI in education and drawing parallels to governance models for other globally deployed technologies, the paper offers a comparative framework for understanding contemporary governance trajectories in AI-enabled educational systems. The analysis argues that regulatory oversight and design-embedded ethical constraints should be treated as complementary layers of governance to support trustworthy, explainable, and equitable AI in education.

title: ROS-Controlled Mobile Manipulation System for Educational and Research Applications: Architecture and Simulation Validation with the MyAGV 2023 Platform
authors: Vladimir Hristov, Antonia De Amorim, Krishan Kumar
publish: 2026 8th International Congress on Human-Computer Interaction, Optimization and Robotic Applications (ICHORA)
abstract: This paper presents a modular software architecture for a compound mobile manipulation system based on the Robot Operating System (ROS). The proposed system integrates the myAGV 2023 mobile robotic platform with a 6 -degree-of-freedom (DOF) manipulator, enabling autonomous navigation and pick-and-place operations. The architecture leverages core ROS concepts-distributed nodes, topic-based messaging, and action servers-to achieve seamless communication between the AGV base and the robotic arm. Key contributions include: (1) a layered architecture separating perception, decision, and actuation; (2) complete implementation of all ROS nodes and launch files; (3) detailed configuration of the navigation stack (move_base with TEB planner) and motion planning (MoveIt! with OMPL); and (4) validation in the Gazebo simulation environment. The system is demonstrated in a simulated pick-and-place scenario. Quantitative experimental validation on physical hardware is planned as future work. The architecture is immediately usable by researchers and educators, and the implementation is available as open-source ROS packages.

title: Advanced Deep Learning for Multi-Modal Mental Health Disorder Detection: A Framework Synthesizing Behavioural, Textual, and Physiological Data
authors: Sanjeeb Kumar Pothal, Aman Pratik Pattnaik, Biswojit Lenka, Aditya Nayak, Adyasha Rath, Ganapati Panda
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Emerging Trends in Advancements and Applications of Computational Intelligence Techniques (ETAACT)
abstract: Mental health disorders are a significant and escalating global health concern. But their diagnosis is mostly based on subjective clinical evaluations and symptoms that they report themselves. Social stigma, delayed reporting, and limited access to mental health professionals can all have an effect on these methods. This frequently results in delayed or inaccurate diagnoses. This paper introduces a comprehensive multimodal deep learning framework for identifying mental health disorders through the simultaneous analysis of textual, behavioral, and physiological data. We use transformer-based language models to figure out what text means and what it means in context. Convolutional neural networks help us learn important things from physiological signals. Deep neural networks model signs of behavior and speech. We use explainable artificial intelligence methods to make clinical settings more open and trustworthy. These give us a clear idea of what the model thinks will happen. Experimental analysis demonstrates that the proposed multimodal fusion approach surpasses single-modality baselines on various evaluation metrics. This indicates its capacity for dependable and credible mental health evaluation.

title: Physical-Layer Security Analysis of Ultra-Dense Small-Cell Networks: An Ergodic Secrecy Rate Perspective with Interference
authors: Thalaimalaichamy. M, Kaviya. M, Bharathi. T, Srikumaran. N, Suba. M
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Trends in Electronics and Informatics (ICOEI)
abstract: Ultra-dense small-cell networks are regarded as a viable option for future wireless communication system capable of improving the spectral efficiency, capacity and coverage through spatial reuse. The ubiquitous wireless transmission susceptibility of hefty networks to tapping calls for physical-layer security analysis. This paper creates a model which considers a system with legitimate users and eavesdroppers in a interference-limited ultra-dense small-cell network. The locations of all BSs, legitimate users, and eavesdroppers are modelled using independent homogeneous Poisson point processes. In addition, fading is achieved via Rayleigh, Rician, and Kernel for realistic channel propagation. The developed closed form expressions for the average main and leakage link rates are backed by simulation efforts. According to the results, increasing the low-density small cell deployment to intermediate and dense deployments significantly improves the ergodic secrecy rate by between 30 and 60%. This is because the transmission distance becomes shorter, while the eavesdroppers experience stronger interference. In contrast, as the user density was enhanced, it was seen that secrecy rate dropped by 10-25% due to an increase of aggregate interference. In contrast, as the density of eavesdroppers gets higher, the performance decreases moderately by 5-15% owing to more information leakage. The findings show that generally, Rician fading conditions produce slightly better secrecy performance than Rayleigh fading because of more reliable main-link. To sum up, findings demonstrate that, in ultra-dense wireless systems, network densification is by nature helpful for improving physical-layer security, while providing useful design insights for secure 5G and beyond communication networks.

title: Progressive Acoustic Stacking–Based Speaker Level System for Pathological Voice Disorder Detection
authors: Bikash Sagar Koiri, Sumit Srivastava
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Emerging Trends in Advancements and Applications of Computational Intelligence Techniques (ETAACT)
abstract: Automatic identification of pathological voice disorders is a crucial task in early clinical diagnosis and objective voice analysis. This experiment presents a Progressive Acoustic Stacking (PAS) approach for pathological voice classification based on complementary spectral and voice quality characteristics. The speech samples are analyzed to extract Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients (MFCCs), Linear Predictive Cepstral Coefficients (LPCCs), formant-related features and voice quality parameters such as jitter, shimmer and harmonic-to-noise ratio. The extracted features are progressively combined to construct increasingly discriminative PAS features. To mitigate inter-speaker variability, a speaker-level decision approach combines posterior probabilities for multiple speech samples. Experimental evaluations on a pathological voice database with functional, neurological, and organic voice disorders show consistent improvement in performance with a speaker-level classification accuracy of 87.32% using a support vector machine classifier. The experimental findings validate the effectiveness of the proposed progressive acoustic stacking approach with speaker-level evaluation for pathological voice disorder identification.

title: Graph-Enhanced LLM Architecture for Reliable Data Management: Design Principles and Proof of Concept
authors: Otmane Azeroual, Radka Nacheva
publish: 2026 8th International Congress on Human-Computer Interaction, Optimization and Robotic Applications (ICHORA)
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) demonstrate significant capabilities in natural language understanding, generation, and reasoning, yet their deployment in structured data contexts is hindered by hallucinations, limited explainability, and unreliable outputs. This paper presents a Graph-Enhanced LLM Architecture that integrates structured knowledge graphs with LLMs to provide traceable, auditable, and explainable responses for AI-assisted data management. The proposed architecture combines natural language interpretation, graph-grounded retrieval, schema-aware validation, and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) in a modular pipeline. Beyond the conceptual architecture, the paper includes a proof-of-concept implementation and a small feasibility demonstration illustrating how graph grounding improves factual support and traceability for representative data management queries. The primary contribution lies in the architectural design principles and integration strategy for graph-enhanced LLM systems. A comprehensive large-scale empirical benchmark evaluation is left for future work.

title: Diet Recommendations for Nutrition Planning Based on Patient Health Indicators Using Random Forest
authors: Nisha Rani D, V G Sivakumar, R Jagadeesh Kannan, RM Sivagama Sundari, Nilamadhab Mishra, Prakash S
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Communication and Signal Processing (ICCSP)
abstract: Personalized nutritional planning is essential for the optimal treatment of lifestyle-related disorders, including diabetes, hypertension, and obesity. Conventional dietary advising methods often depend on broad principles and fail to assess complex connections among many health markers. This research introduces an intelligent dietary recommendation system using a Random Forest (RF) classifier to provide personalized meal plans based on individual health profiles. The model is trained and assessed using the Kaggle Diet Recommendations Dataset, including 1000 patient records with 17 attributes, encompassing demographic information, clinical indicators, dietary practices, physical activity levels, and illness severity. The goal variable classifies diet plans into Low-Carb, Low-Sodium, and Balanced categories. Before model training, data pretreatment methods including categorical variable encoding, feature scaling, and missing value management were used. The RF model adeptly identifies non-linear associations among health markers, including BMI, blood glucose, blood pressure, cholesterol levels, and nutritional imbalance scores. The experimental findings indicate that the proposed model attains an overall classification accuracy of 97.8%, exhibiting great precision and recall across all dietary categories. Analysis of feature significance indicates that illness type, BMI, glucose level, blood pressure, and dietary nutrient imbalance scores are the predominant variables influencing diet recommendation decisions. The findings validate the efficacy and appropriateness of the RF methodology for individualized nutrition planning and underscore its prospective use in clinical decision support and preventive healthcare systems.

title: Predictive Modeling for Early Detection and Classification of Cardiovascular Diseases Using Logistic Regression
authors: Geetha R, Santhanakrishnan R, T.G. Ramabharathi, S Sujatha, Priya Sengupta, Prakash S
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Communication and Signal Processing (ICCSP)
abstract: Cardiovascular diseases (CVDs) are among the leading causes of death globally, necessitating prompt detection and intervention. Precise and comprehensible predictive models can aid healthcare practitioners in recognizing high-risk patients and enhancing clinical decision-making. This research introduces a logistic regression (LR) model for the early identification and classification of heart disease. The model is trained on a dataset containing pertinent clinical and demographic variables to successfully differentiate between the presence and absence of heart disease. The proposed method achieved an overall accuracy of 88.5 %, indicating its robust predictive efficacy. LR provides interpretability, allowing healthcare providers to comprehend the impact of each feature on illness prediction. The model's efficacy highlights the capacity of machine learning predictive tools to facilitate early diagnosis, inform preventive measures, and enhance patient outcomes. These results highlight the practicality of utilizing statistical learning models in clinical environments, providing a dependable and transparent approach for the early diagnosis of cardiac disease. Future research may investigate the incorporation of supplementary patient data, feature engineering, or hybrid modelling methodologies to further improve prediction efficacy and therapeutic relevance.

title: Stability Enhancement of CNTFET Based Compute-In-Memory SRAM Systems
authors: Imaya S, Mathan N
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Communication and Signal Processing (ICCSP)
abstract: This paper presents a detailed stability analysis of Compute-in-Memory (CIM) Static Random Access Memory (SRAM) using Carbon Nanotube Field-Effect Transistor (CNTFET) technology. The study aims to enhance the stability of CIM-SRAM cells by integrating advanced circuit techniques and analyzing various performance metrics such as Static Noise Margin (SNM), Read/Write Margin, and Power Dissipation. A comparative analysis between CNTFET-based and conventional MOSFET-based SRAM structures is performed to evaluate the advantages of CNTFET in terms of power efficiency, speed, and reliability. The proposed design includes an optimized write driver to improve data retention and security. The simulation results, obtained using Synopsys HSpice and COSMOSCOPE, demonstrate significant improvements in stability and performance.

title: A Hybrid Deep Learning Framework using Tab Transformer and Light GBM For Enhanced Network Intrusion Detection
authors: Nandhini S, Krishnapriya D, Nashiha Sulthana G, Dhanushiya R, Chandru C, Eashwaran S
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Trends in Electronics and Informatics (ICOEI)
abstract: Detecting cyber attacks has become increasingly intricate as network traffic continues to grow in both volume and complexity, often causing decreased detection precision and an increase in false positives. To address these challenges, this work introduces a multi-model deep learning framework that combines the functionalities of a Denoising Autoencoder, SMOTE, TabTransformer, and LightGBM. In this model, the Autoencoder filters out noise and improves the representation of numerical features, whereas SMOTE maintains dataset balance by producing synthetic instances for minority attack classes. The TabTransformer effectively captures meaningful relationships among categorical attributes, and LightGBM delivers fast, efficient, and accurate classification. Together, these techniques build a robust and adaptable system that can precisely differentiate between normal and malicious network behavior. The model was evaluated on the CICIDS2017 dataset recording an overall accuracy of 97.4%, with precision, recall, and F1-scores all surpassing 95%. These outcomes emphasize how the integration of advanced feature learning, balanced data handling, and attention-based modeling contributes to a dependable and efficient system for detecting intrusions in real-time across modern cybersecurity environments.

title: Intelligent Framework for Traffic Accident Risk Prediction Using Machine Learning
authors: Dipesh Dalmia, Akshit Saini, Aman Tyagi, Reshma Saluja, Kalpana Sangwan, Poonam Kukana
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Emerging Trends in Advancements and Applications of Computational Intelligence Techniques (ETAACT)
abstract: Traffic accidents are still a serious concern in terms of road safety, requiring proactive and intelligent prediction systems. In this paper, an AI-driven real-time traffic accident risk prediction system based on big data analysis and machine learning is proposed. The system combines information from various sources, including traffic sensors, GPS trajectories, weather information, and road infrastructure data, to produce predictive risk scores. We tested it on real-time data for six months, including 2.5 million traffic records, 500,000 GPS-tracked movements, and 100,000 incident reports. In terms of performance, the Random Forest algorithm had an accuracy of 92.3%, and the Deep Neural Network had an accuracy of 94.7%. The system effectively located 87% of the high-risk accident areas in a five-minute prediction interval. Other evaluation criteria include precision, recall, F1-score, training time, and inference time, which show the system’s robustness and efficiency. Real-time system implementation resulted in a 29.5% decrease in accidents and a 40% improvement in response time compared to existing systems.

title: Artificial Intelligence for Merging Modalities in Medical Imaging: A Fusion-Based Enhancement Approach
authors: Gurnoor Singh Dang, Majid Rodgar, Michael Snyder
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Communication and Signal Processing (ICCSP)
abstract: Modern medical imaging plays a central role in diagnostics, yet each modality carries unique strengths alongside unavoidable limitations. Drawing on insights from Stanford's Mini-Fellowship in Molecular Imaging Techniques, this study introduces FusionScan, a novel framework that unites Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) and Computed Tomography (CT) with an AI-driven enhancement system. The goal is to evaluate whether this combination can achieve greater depth, resolution, and functional detail by harnessing the complementary advantages of both modalities, offering a more comprehensive solution for complex diagnostic needs. The approach involves merging MRI and CT data and applying AI algorithms to refine resolution and increase molecular specificity. Particular emphasis is placed on the challenge of multimodal alignment, with strategies proposed to ensure precise registration between imaging outputs. Publicly available medical imaging datasets will serve as the testing ground for assessing how AI improves reconstruction, resolution, and adaptive adjustment of imaging parameters. An integrated feedback mechanism is also planned, allowing the system to modify scanning parameters in real time based on preliminary AI assessments. This feature is designed to minimize radiation exposure, shorten scan durations, and concentrate detail on regions requiring closer examination. Ultimately, this research envisions the development of a fused, AIenhanced architecture capable of delivering superior diagnostic insight, supported by simulations that illustrate the transformative impact of AI on medical imaging.

title: Wi-Fi 7 as a Passive Radar: Decimeter-Level Human Activity Recognition for Healthcare and Security
authors: Thirugnana Sambandham P, Vijayakumar K, S Devapriya, K Suganya, Karthikeyan P, Elamathi N
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Communication and Signal Processing (ICCSP)
abstract: This paper presents a novel passive sensing system that leverages the advanced features of Wi-Fi 7 to achieve decimeter-level human activity recognition, offering a robust solution for healthcare monitoring and security. By harnessing Channel State Information (CSI) from commercially available Wi-Fi 7 routers operating on a 320 MHz bandwidth, we extracted ultra-fine-grained wireless channel perturbations caused by human movement. Our key contributions include: (1) a novel signal processing pipeline that exploits Wi-Fi 7's increased spectral and spatial resolution to achieve decimeterlevel tracking precision (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$<15 ~\text{cm}$</tex>) and distinguish closely spaced multiple subjects; (2) a hybrid deep learning model for highaccuracy (>98%) recognition of critical activities such as falls, ambulation, and intrusion attempts; and (3) a comprehensive privacy-preserving framework, as the system requires no wearable devices or cameras, analyzing only abstract RF channel data. Experimental validation in real-world environments demonstrated superior performance compared to state-of-the-art Wi-Fi 6-based systems, particularly in throughwall scenarios and multi-occupant settings. This study establishes Wi-Fi 7 as a powerful, ubiquitous, and non-intrusive platform for next-generation passive sensing.

title: Quantum Key Distribution (QKD)- Based Cloud Security Model Integrated with Quantum Machine Learning(QML) for Threat Detection
authors: Tummalacheruvu Rajesh, Mukkamala Narasimha Swamy, Guru Vimal Kumar Murugan
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Communication and Signal Processing (ICCSP)
abstract: Fears regarding the security of any data are increasing drastically with the rapid deployment of cloud-based systems based on artificial intelligence (AI) systems, as of now, owing to the existence of quantum computing, which is threatening the existing cryptography schemes. To ensure secure communication and smart threat detection in cloud networks, this paper proposes a hybrid cloud-based security framework, Quantum Shield, which will deploy Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) and Quantum Machine Learning (QML). The QKD module relies on the BB84 protocol when generating encryption keys, sharing the Quantum Bit Error Rate (QBER) to detect eavesdropping and is considered to be safe. At the same time, the QML layer manipulates TensorFlow Quantum-executed Variational Quantum Circuits (VQC) to perform the processes of classification on encrypted data. QKD and QML modules are also scalably deployed on cloud infrastructure and the system is evaluated and measured within a simulated environment. The experiments suggest the framework is effective in identifying interception attacks and containing QBER below the security threshold in case of normal conditions. It also classifies better than its traditional counterparts. Further studies will be focused on quantum operation and the validation on a large scale. The proposed approach demonstrates a quantum and scalable approach to protecting cloud AI systems.

title: Decision Support System for Optimizing Rake Formation Strategies
authors: Achint Kumar, Sidhant Das, Madan Bag, Rasmita Dash
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Emerging Trends in Advancements and Applications of Computational Intelligence Techniques (ETAACT)
abstract: SAIL and other steel producers spend a large part of their budget, approximately 14 to 15 percent, on transportation. So, if they are not properly assembling the rakes, it is a significant issue that needs to be addressed as soon as possible. The real problem is that, For now, they are still doing the dispatch planning, and manually entering the data into the system. This makes the whole process longer, which leads to space constraints, order delays, and additional costs. This paper discusses a tool called DSS-RakeOpt. that is designed to improve efficiency. It functions as a Decision Support System that integrates Mixed-Integer Linear Programming (MILP) and predictive learning to improve how steel is delivered from the steelmaking plant to warehouses and respective customers. It removes uncertainty. The system analyzes all variables - including rolling stock placement, customer orders and available inventory to create the most efficient daily rake formation schedule possible and it provides better results. When tested with discrete event simulation, the system allocates better resource use and reduces the costly delayed charges.

title: GC-GAN: An Adversarial Attack Model for CPSS With Bipartite Graphs and Cooptimization Mechanisms
authors: Kai Zhong, Siyang Yu, Zhibang Yang, Shuangyu Liu, Kenli Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems
abstract: Cyber–physical–social systems (CPSSs) integrate the physical environment, information networks, and social interactions, serving as critical infrastructures in domains such as energy, transportation, and healthcare. While the multimodal and multilayered architecture of CPSS fosters innovation and operational efficiency, it simultaneously introduces significant security vulnerabilities. The high degree of interconnectivity inherent in CPSS makes them particularly susceptible to adversarial attacks, positioning them as high-risk targets. Developing effective attack strategies that leverage the unique data characteristics of CPSS, as well as establishing verifiable experimental frameworks, has become a prominent research focus. However, existing studies have not adequately addressed the development of robust attack models under limited data availability, resulting in problems such as unsatisfactory attack success rates, poor adversarial training, and insufficient attack stealth. To address these challenges, this article proposes a novel adversarial attack model, GC-GAN, which is based on generative adversarial networks (GANs). The GC-GAN framework comprises three core components: a generator, an authenticity discriminator, and an attack trainer. The generator employs the bipartite graph attribute-sharing theory and integrates graph and sequence encoders to extract latent patterns from known datasets. The discriminator and attack trainer are collaboratively trained to iteratively refine the generated samples by balancing authenticity with attack objectives. This collaborative mechanism enhances attack success rates while minimizing the risk of detection. Extensive experimental evaluations validate the efficacy of the proposed model. Compared to existing approaches, GC-GAN demonstrates significant improvements in both attack success rate and stealthiness.

title: Bimodal Retinal Image Fusion Using CMPE-Enhanced Swin Transformer
authors: Ojas Shrivastava, Neeraj Kumar Pandey, Manoj Diwakar, Sanjay Roka, Aditya Joshi, Prabhishek Singh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Systems in Engineering, Secured Systems and Cybersecurity (ICISESSC)
abstract: The automated classification of retinal diseases is becoming an increasingly popular area of research as the world wide incidence of sight-threatening eye disorders continues to grow while there is a shortage of trained ophthalmologists to diagnose and treat these conditions. Fundus photographs provide the most valuable vascular information about the surface of the retina, and Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT) provides a much more detailed cross-sectional view of each layer in the retina. While the majority of current deep learning methods are based only on one type of data (i.e., they do not take advantage of the complementary properties of both types of images), there are some that use both types of data but do not utilize the full capabilities of each. The authors of this work propose a new bimodal deep learning framework based on the Cross-Modality Swin Transformer design for the classification of retinal diseases. By employing the cross-modality embedding method, the authors were able to fuse the features of both types of images at the time of training using a modified Swin Transformer backbone. Furthermore, a new Cross-Modality Positional Encoding module has been added to the transformer architecture so as to maintain the modality-specific contextual information in the transformer latent space. To evaluate the performance of this proposed approach, the authors conducted experiments on the OLIVES retinal dataset with a stratified train-validation split. The results of these experiments demonstrate that the method generates superior classification performance results compared with unimodal and conventional CNNs, thus validating the use of multimodal fusion with transformer networks for retinal disease diagnosis.

title: Research on Exercise Recommendation Based on Knowledge Graph and Dynamic Policy Learning
authors: Leqi Li, Xiaoxiao Wang, Wenfeng Chen
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: Traditional knowledge graph-based exercise recommendation methods primarily rely on static semantic matching, often neglecting the evolution of students’ dynamic cognitive states during the learning process. To address this limitation, this paper proposes a Knowledge Graph-enhanced Adaptive Dueling Double Deep Q-Network (KGA-D3QN) recommendation model. The model formulates the exercise recommendation task as a Markov Decision Process (MDP), aiming to maximize long-term cumulative pedagogical returns. Experimental results on the Algebra 2005 dataset demonstrate that the proposed method significantly enhances students’ knowledge states while maintaining competitive accuracy and recommendation diversity.

title: An Intelligent Energy Management Framework for Cost-Efficient Renewable Power Utilization in Smart Homes
authors: Swapnesh Taterh, Ashish Kumar Rastogi, Arun Kumar Singh, Yamini Sharma, Mukesh Kumar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Systems in Engineering, Secured Systems and Cybersecurity (ICISESSC)
abstract: With conventional forms of energy, especially coal, slowly getting exhausted, the whole world seems to be taking a spurt toward finding alternative forms of energy from renewables. The energies derived from the sun and wind have enormous potential, which remains unexplored even now. If harnessed optimally, they have the potential to provide a large chunk of energy for the world's increasing demand for electricity. Recently, advances in artificial intelligence and neural networks have made great strides toward embedding renewable energy into intelligent energy management systems that optimize consumption to reduce total cost. This paper outlines an innovatively designed energy management system for domestic energy consumption with embedded solar PV, wireless power transmission, and AI consumption pattern learning methods. The framework, under design, allows proportional utilizations of the solar energy generated at home while functional loading patterns are ascertained in real time within the home. It is designed to be functional independent of net grid consumption and generally aims to affect energy efficiency, reduced reliance on conventional sources, and lower electricity cost.

title: P3LiteFormer: An Edge-Optimized YOLO-Transformer Hybrid for Small Object Detection in UAV Traffic Monitoring
authors: Amanda Gozho, Lin Bai
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: Small object detection in UAV traffic imagery remains challenging because of limited resolution, dense scenes, and onboard computational constraints. To address this issue, this paper proposes P3LiteFormer, a lightweight YOLO-Transformer hybrid that introduces a Transformer encoder only at the high- resolution P3 feature layer of YOLOv8s. This scale-specific design enhances global contextual modeling for small objects while adding only 2.7% parameters and 3.1% more FLOPs. On the VisDrone2019 dataset, the proposed method achieves an mAP@0.5 of 0.404, improving the baseline YOLOv8s by 21.7%, with 19.1% gains in both precision and recall. These results show that P3LiteFormer provides an effective and efficient solution for real-time UAV edge deployment.

title: Cardiac 3D Mechanical and Electrical Signal Reconstruction via Defocused Speckle Imaging
authors: Ming Xia, Lin Liu, Ningbo Zhao, Zi Luo, Caifeng Shan, Wenjin Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Biomedical Engineering
abstract: Defocused speckle imaging (DSI) enables non-contact measurement of mechanocardiography (MCG) signals, such as seismocardiography (SCG) and gyrocardiography (GCG). As cardiac function is inherently governed by the coupling between mechanical motion and electrical excitation, this intrinsic relationship suggests that mechanical signals captured by DSI may further support the inference of electrical activities, i.e., the electrocardiogram (ECG). Therefore, we propose CardioNet, a physics-driven framework that leverages speckle motion signals together with physical model-based priors to reconstruct cardiac mechanical and electrical activities simultaneously. By coupling the MCG representations with the synchronized ECG, CardioNet captures the intrinsic electromechanical relationship of the heart, enabling non-contact and comprehensive cardiac monitoring. CardioNet was evaluated in a cross-subject study involving 20 laboratory subjects and 30 clinical patients, yielding Pearson correlation coefficients of 0.718/0.911 (SCG/ECG) in laboratory settings and 0.725/0.850 in clinical settings. Furthermore, it accurately captures fine-grained biomarkers, achieving mean timing errors of only 1.36/1.39 ms (lab) and 2.20/2.27 ms (clinic) for detecting aortic valve opening events in MCG signals and R-peaks in ECG signals, with similarly low timing errors for other hemodynamics-related markers. While promising for rhythm analysis, capturing subtle morphologies and spatial dynamics remains challenging. Future work will target 12-lead spatial reconstruction and broader pathological cohorts. To facilitate reproducibility, our dataset has been released at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/contactless-healthcare/CardioNet</uri>

title: A Novel Framework and Dataset for Subtle Hand and Finger Movement Recognition in Workflow Analysis
authors: Aiman Khan Nazir, Lanfang Dong, Bei Hua, Qasim Jan, Kang Dong, Xiahan Feng
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: Subtle hand-centric activity recognition is important for workflow analysis across various domains, such as analyzing daily office behavior, monitoring the standardization of assembly line operations, and enabling automated grading of students’ laboratory experiments. Consequently, this paper presents a novel Subtle Hand-Centric Activity Recognition with Duration Estimation (SHRDE) framework, which facilitates workflow analysis and productivity assessments. The SHRDE utilizes a sliding-window mechanism on MediaPipe streams and a skeleton-based activity recognition model. SHRDE continuously recognizes activities, their transition, and measures duration, which helps to analyze the workflow and individuals’ performance. Moreover, we introduce a new multi-view office activity dataset (OA5V4) to support fine-grained recognition of hand and finger movements. It covers 5 activities, which are captured across 4 viewpoints. SHRDE is evaluated on unseen subjects (i.e., cross-subject validation), and an accuracy of 97.33% is achieved. Moreover, it is also validated across different postures, lighting conditions, occlusion, and multi-person settings, demonstrating its robustness in complex scenarios.<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup>

title: An Interpretable Efficient Framework for Real-Time Pearl Millet Disease Detection
authors: Sanjeev Thakur, Seema Rao, Dibyajyoty Beura, Kanchan Galiyan, Omdev Dahiya, Tanima Thakur, Amin Renees M V
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Systems in Engineering, Secured Systems and Cybersecurity (ICISESSC)
abstract: The pearl millet crop is highly nutritious, resistant to drought and is under a great threat of fungal infections such as the blast and rust, which may cause up to forty percent of the crop to be lost. The conventional types of diagnosis are typically founded on the time-taking process and manual one, on the experience of the professionals, which makes it inapplicable to the large scale agriculture. The proposed solution is of the state of the art, as the current work proposes: automated smart framework, which would be inefficient to monitor the millet. The model uses the EfficientNetB0 architecture that is enhanced with an Enhanced Predictive Intelligence (EPI) attention mechanism. Both channel- and spatially-based attention have been incorporated into the EPI module to accurately identify disease patterns and their precise loci on the surfaces of leaves, producing unambiguous and understandable results. The training of a dataset containing 2,847 carefully hand-labeled images, both healthy and infected, gave impressive performance statistics, with 95.79 percent accuracy, 95.82 percent precision, and 95.80 percent F1-score. Grad-CAM visualization is also more effective in improving the interpretability of models by illustrating the main influential area. Having a small size of 18.5MB and a latency of inference of 75 ms, the model can run on Internet-of-Things devices with ease, which is why it can be deployed on the edge in sustainable farming settings. This kind of implementation will enable early and focused interventions, which may in turn decrease the use of chemical pesticides by a third to a half, and the yield losses of about forty percent. The presented study explains the capacity of interpretable, deployable deep-learning solutions on resource-constrained hardware, thus developing precision agriculture and, in line with the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals, especially those related to Zero Hunger, Climate Action, and Responsible Production.

title: Effective Drive Current and Capacitance Models for NCFET-based Ring Oscillator
authors: Jungsu Kim, Changhwan Shin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems
abstract: Accurate prediction of AC performance in negative capacitance field effect transistor (NCFET) circuits require effective modeling approaches that capture their unique electrical characteristics. This work proposes enhanced effective drive current (I<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">EFF3</sub> = [1.25I<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">H</sub> + I<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">L</sub> + I<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">M</sub> + 0.375I<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">DLIN</sub>]/3) and capacitance (C<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">EFF_delay</sub>) models for analyzing and predicting the AC performance of ring oscillators (ROs) based on both NCFETs and baseline MOSFETs. The proposed I<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">EFF3</sub> model extends conventional approaches by incorporating an intermediate current component, resulting in improved correlation between DC effective resistance and AC resistance of the RO. Mixed-mode device and circuit simulations validate the model’s accuracy across various operating conditions. Compared to the previous model, i.e., I<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">EFF</sub>+ ([1.15I<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">H</sub> + I<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">L</sub>+ 0.25I<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">DLIN</sub>]/2), the proposed approach significantly reduces the mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) from 9.8% to 1.4% and decreases the maximum error rate from 34.1% to 6.4%. Furthermore, this work addresses the unique capacitance-voltage characteristics of NCFETs, which exhibit peak capacitance (C<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">peak</sub>) at gate voltages below the operating voltage. The C<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">EFF_delay</sub> model employs an average capacitance concept based on voltage-dependent gate charge variations. The analytical delay calculation, combining R<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">EFF3_delay</sub> and C<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">EFF_delay</sub>, demonstrates excellent agreement with RO simulation results, achieving a MAPE of 1.7% and a maximum error rate of 5.7%.

title: AECPP: Active Electrolocation System with Cooperative Path Planning for Micro-Small Underwater Vehicles
authors: Teng Ma, Jingyi Quan, Luting Lv, Jiajia Fan, Hongyu Sun, Qiang Zhang, Wenjun Zhang, Ye Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Micro-small autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs) typically rely on micro-electromechanical systems (MEMS) inertial measurement units (IMUs) for dead reckoning navigation, yet their navigation accuracy is compromised by cumulative errors over time. Considering the payload space, cost, and environmental compatibility, underwater active electrolocation offers an effective complementary sensing modality to mitigate this issue. This paper proposes a moving target tracking method that integrates active electrolocation-based quantum-behaved particle swarm optimization (QPSO) localization and rapidly-exploring random tree<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">*</sup> (RRT<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">*</sup>) path planning algorithm, enabling accurate underwater tracking and localization of moving targets. Tank experiments were conducted using the proposed method. Results demonstrate that the AECPP system achieves robust tracking under different planned paths and target velocities, and the average locating error was reduced by 27.43% compared with the least square (LS) algorithm.

title: GVHMR: Gravity-View Coordinates for Global Human Motion Recovery From Monocular Videos_supp1-3699758.pdf
authors: Sida Peng
publish: N/A
abstract: We present a novel method for recovering world-grounded human motion from monocular video. The main challenge lies in the ambiguity of defining the world coordinate system, which varies between sequences. Previous approaches attempt to alleviate this issue by predicting relative motion in an auto-regressive manner, but are prone to accumulating errors. Instead, we propose estimating human poses in a novel Gravity-View (GV) coordinate system, which is defined by the world gravity and the camera view direction. The proposed GV system is naturally gravity-aligned and uniquely defined for each video frame, largely reducing the ambiguity of learning image-pose mapping. The estimated poses can be transformed back to the world coordinate system using camera rotations, forming a global motion sequence. Additionally, the per-frame estimation avoids error accumulation in the auto-regressive methods. Experiments on in-the-wild benchmarks demonstrate that our method recovers more realistic motion in both the camera space and world-grounded settings, outperforming state-of-the-art methods in both accuracy and speed. We further introduce a Stationary Label Predictor (SLP) to estimate the motion stationary states of hands and feet. By producing sharper decision boundaries, the module mitigates unnatural artifacts such as foot-sliding and shows improved performance under fast-motion inputs. The code is available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://zju3dv.github.io/gvhmr</uri>.

title: Vector-Space Global Collocation Method for Modeling Rod-Driven Parallel Continuum Manipulators_supp2-3694012.mp4
authors: Rui Chen
publish: N/A
abstract: Parallel continuum manipulators (PCMs) have attracted increasing attention owing to their flexibility, high precision, and strong load-bearing capacity, compared with their single-arm continuum counterparts. In this article, a kinetostatic modeling framework for PCMs is developed based on Cosserat rod theory, and a vector-space global collocation method (GCM) is proposed to solve the derived model. The GCM selects appropriate basis functions to approximate the unknown functions in vector space under different loading and actuation conditions. In particular, variables originally defined on the Lie group, such as rotation matrices, are reformulated entirely in the vector space while preserving orthogonality, thereby avoiding complex mathematical and numerical operations on the manifold. The proposed method is validated through FEM and experimental tests on a 3-rod PCM prototype. The maximum relative error in the rotation angle is 2.69%, and the absolute error in the end-effector height variation is 1.1 mm, corresponding to 0.55% of its initial height. Finally, a conceptual flexible vector thruster is demonstrated, within which a PCM-based thruster serves as a core component. The kinetostatic and compliance characteristics of this PCM-based thruster are analyzed using the proposed GCM-based semianalytical framework highlighting the simplicity, efficiency, and robustness of the developed framework.

title: C-PASS: Center-Fed Pinching Antenna System
authors: Xu Gan, Yuanwei Liu
publish: IEEE Wireless Communications Letters
abstract: A novel architecture of the center-fed pinching antenna system (C-PASS) is proposed. In contrast to the conventional end-fed PASS, signals are fed from the center input ports and propagate towards both sides of the waveguide. By doing so, spatial-multiplexing gain can be achieved in a single waveguide. Based on the proposed C-PASS, closed-form expressions for the degree of freedom (DoF) and power scaling laws are derived. These theoretical results reveal that C-PASS can achieve twice the DoF and an additional multiplexing gain of O(PT ln4 N/N2) compared to the conventional PASS, where PT and 2N represent the transmit power and the total pinching antenna number, respectively. Numerical results are provided to demonstrate that substantial capacity improvements can be achieved through the enhanced DoF and multiplexing gain of the C-PASS.

title: Promoting Emission Reduction of Coupled Power-Traffic Networks Based on Energy-Carbon Integrated Prices
authors: Wenjie Qiao, Ge Guo, Ning Zhang, Yinghua Han, Fangyuan Si, Qiang Zhao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: The adoption of electric vehicles for global decarbonization integrates traffic and power networks. However, emissions from upstream power generation and ongoing gasoline vehicles continue to impact overall carbon levels, which cannot be ignored. This work addresses this issue by proposing a uniform carbon pricing scheme, where the emission responsibilities are incorporated to achieve low-carbon scheduling of coupled power-traffic networks. Specifically, gasoline vehicles have a direct carbon price imposition through an emission model; the carbon emission flow theory enables a carbon price based on the actual charging demand, thus, an innovative energy-carbon integrated price is introduced to regulate electric vehicles. The scheduling results are obtained by an iterative method, with their existence and uniqueness proven through the fixed-point theory, and a Genetic Algorithm-based method is introduced to accelerate the computation process. Finally, two case studies demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed pricing scheme in identifying emission responsibilities and imposing charging fees on emissions. The outcomes demonstrate a superior balance between reducing emissions and enhancing economic performance compared to current methods.

title: Condition-Aware Progressive Knowledge Distillation: Lightweight and Accurate Prediction of Lithium-Ion Battery Remaining Useful Life
authors: Penghua Li, Ao Chen, Yunhong Che, Yangming Zhang, Jingjing Zhou, Xinmin Zhang, Guodong Wang, Xiaosong Hu, Schahram Dustdar
publish: IEEE Transactions on Transportation Electrification
abstract: This study presents a condition-aware progressive knowledge distillation (KD) framework for developing lightweight models capable of accurately predicting the remaining useful life (RUL) of lithium-ion batteries. Within this framework, a teacher model is constructed by embedding a conditional variational autoencoder (CVAE) into a Mamba backbone. Through high-order compression and reconstruction of local–global battery features and the adoption of a dependency-aware loss function, the latent space is regularized to capture the underlying structure of degradation trajectories, enabling high-precision RUL prediction. Subsequently, a lightweight student predictor, realised as a CVAE enriched with operating-condition descriptors, is configured as the target model. Unsupervised clustering is employed in conjunction with a dual-path gating mechanism to activate an operating-condition monitor. While a multi-channel topology constructed from depthwise separable convolutions is triggered under the synergistic action of the gating weights to extract discriminative features across varying operating regimes and to deepen the representation of complex degradation dynamics. To transfer domain knowledge, a stepwise parameter-freezing KD strategy is devised whereby accuracy-controlled degradation knowledge is progressively distilled from the teacher, ensuring effective RUL prediction by the compact student. Validation indicates that the distilled lightweight model achieves average root-mean-square errors of 0.0163 on the MIT dataset and 0.0384 on a real-world EV dataset, while using only 0.167M parameters and requiring 13.88 ms per inference on embedded hardware. The code and models are available at https://github.com/Lipenghua-CQ/CPKD.

title: The Trust Paradox in LLM-Based Multi-Agent Systems: When Collaboration Becomes a Security Vulnerability
authors: Zijie Xu, Minfeng Qi, Shiqing Wu, Lefeng Zhang, Qiwen Wei, Han He, Ningran Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems
abstract: Multi-agent systems powered by large language models (LLMs) are advancing rapidly, yet the security implications of trust-mediated collaboration remain insufficiently understood. We introduce and empirically validate the trust–vulnerability paradox (TVP): increasing inter-agent trust to improve coordination can simultaneously amplify risks of over-exposure and authorization drift (AD). To study this phenomenon under controlled and reproducible conditions, we operationalize trust as a system-level control parameter governing authorization strictness and information disclosure, rather than as a cognitive or social construct. We construct a purpose-built scenario-game dataset spanning three macro-scenes and nineteen sub-scenes, and conduct extensive closed-loop agent-to-agent (A2A) interactions with explicit trust parameterization. Using minimum necessary information (MNI) as the safety baseline, we propose two unified metrics: over-exposure rate (OER) to quantify boundary violations, and AD to capture the sensitivity of disclosure behavior to trust variation. Experiments across multiple model backends and orchestration frameworks reveal consistent trends: higher trust improves task success but also increases over-exposure risk, with substantial heterogeneity in risk sensitivity to trust across systems. We further evaluate mitigation strategies, including Sensitive Information Repartitioning and guardian-agent (GA-Agent) enablement, which effectively reduce OER and attenuate AD under elevated trust. Overall, this work formalizes TVP as a systemic security property, establishes reproducible experimental baselines with unified metrics, and demonstrates that trust must be modelled, calibrated, and managed as a first-class security variable in the design of LLM-based multi-agent systems (LLM-MAS).

title: Measuring the Topological Charge of Infrared Vortices via Nonlinear Interferometry
authors: Yating Zeng, Qian Wen, Huaixi Chen, Zeyu Yan, Hua Zheng, Xiaoqin Zhu, Yuanying Zhang
publish: IEEE Photonics Technology Letters
abstract: We demonstrate a nonlinear Mach-Zehnder interferometer (MZI) designed for direct characterization of infrared (IR) optical vortices. By interfering an IR signal vortex with a dynamically phase-modulated reference beam, the MZI maps IR vortex modes into visible second-harmonic generation (SHG) interference patterns. Distinct from linear interferometers, the nonlinear characteristic of our MZI provides an inherent contrast enhancement, where the quadratic intensity response effectively sharpens the petal structures to improve fringe visibility significantly. These petal-like interference patterns allow for the simultaneous retrieval of the topological charge (TC) magnitude and sign, which are determined directly from the number of petals and rotation direction, respectively. By leveraging the high sensitivity of standard visible-light CMOS sensors, our scheme provides an efficient platform for IR vortex metrology and OAM-based optical communications.

title: Design and Analysis of Compensation Networks in Resonant Inductive Power Transfer Accounting for Parasitic Effects
authors: Alberto Dolara, Marco Biasizzo, Emanuele Ogliari, Sonia Leva, Delia Guarnaschelli
publish: IEEE Journal on Wireless Power Technologies
abstract: Compensation networks play a critical role in Resonant Inductive Wireless Power Transfer systems, as their proper design enhances both power transmission and overall efficiency. This paper proposes a systematic approach for sizing the elements of the compensation networks based on a defined set of design targets and a hybrid symbolic–numerical methodology that inherently accounts for the dissipative effects of both the pads and the compensation network components. Three modeling criteria are introduced to quantify the influence of parasitic resistances associated with the circuit elements. Four different compensation topologies, suitable for electric vehicle wireless chargers, are analyzed, each featuring an increased number of circuit elements. Simulation results confirm the effectiveness of the proposed tuning methodology and highlight how dissipative effects impact both the efficiency and dynamic behavior of the compensated coreless transformer.

title:
MoS
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    :PVA-based Flexible Optoelectronic Reconfigurable Memristor for Neuromorphic Computing
authors: Harsh Ranjan, Parthasarathi Pal, Vivek Pratap Singh, Gulshan Kumar, Sanjay Kumar, Saurabh Kumar Pandey
publish: IEEE Electron Device Letters
abstract: This letter presents a flexible Ag/MoS2:PVA/SiO2/Pt memristor featuring controllable transition between volatile self-rectifying threshold switching (SRTS) and non-volatile bipolar resistive switching dependent on compliance current (CC) and operating voltage. The device exhibits a high rectification ratio (∼ 103) and ultrafast relaxation (∼170 ns) time in SRTS mode, functioning as an effective selector. In non-volatile mode, it exhibits stable multi-level storage and persistence photoconductivity (PPC) under a red light source, enabling optoelectronic synaptic emulation. Integrated NeurosimV 3.0 hardware simulation reveals weight updates (NLP/D ≈ 4.21/ − 4.86) functionality and achieves 85% MNIST handwritten dataset classification accuracy. These results provide a robust, single-layer optoelectronic memristive hardware platform for integrated sensing, cell selection, and neuromorphic computing in flexible electronics.

title:
A 58.9—73.7-GHz Quadrature Dual-Core Class-F
                    <sub>3,5</sub>
                    Oscillator with Tail-Assisted Implicit Quintupling
authors: Yevhenii Mormul, Patchara Sawakewang, Sayan Kumar, Chawin Khongprasongiri, Teerachot Siriburanon, Robert Bogdan Staszewski
publish: IEEE Solid-State Circuits Letters
abstract: We introduce a new class-F3,5 oscillator topology that enables quadrature operation by duplicating a conventional class-F3 oscillator core and electro-magnetically coupling the two cores through a pair of additional coils feeding source terminals of the gm transistors. The coupling network enhances the 5th-harmonic component, enabling efficient extraction and quadrature operation across the 58.9—73.7-GHz mm-wave frequency range (22.6%). Fabricated in 22nm FD-SOI, the class-F3,5 oscillator achieves an average phase noise of –124.4 dBc/Hz at a 10MHz offset, resulting in 188/195 dB FoM/FoMT.

title: Reachability-Constrained Motion Planning and Control Integration Framework for DDEVs: A Forward Set Propagation Method
authors: Fanxun Wang, Guodong Yin, Yanbo Lu, Tong Shen, Ang Li, Yanjun Ren, Ruiqi Fang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: The integration of motion planning and control within a unified architecture based on reachability theory provides an effective approach to extending the autonomous motion capability boundaries of distributed drive electric vehicles (DDEVs). This architecture is further enhanced by embedding forward set propagation methods into the dynamics analysis of DDEVs. The analytical expression of forward reachable sets (FRSs), the real-time processing of reachability constraints, and the design of integrated planning-control frameworks remain challenging. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a reachability-constrained motion planning and control integration framework (RC-MPCI) for DDEVs. The proposed RC-MPCI features strong interpretability, rigorous safety guarantees, and computational efficiency. First, a maneuver-oriented vehicle motion model is established to construct the closed-loop system dynamics. Subsequently, an FRS computation method based on sum-of-squares programming (SOSP) is proposed. It formulates an analytical expression of dynamic reachable boundaries that accounts for multi-actuator coordination, and tracking error models are introduced to ensure the reachability of the closed- loop system. Then, a constraint optimization strategy based on collision-free tunnels (CFTs) is designed, within which online motion planning and control methods are developed under a receding horizon optimization framework. Finally, the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed RC-MPCI are confirmed through virtual simulations and hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) tests. This framework, based on reachability theory, provides both theoretical and technical foundations that enable safe and efficient autonomous motion of DDEVs in highly dynamic environments.

title: A High-Order Discretization Scheme for Surface Integral Equations for Analyzing the Electroencephalography Forward Problem
authors: Rui Chen, Viviana Giunzioni, Adrien Merlini, Francesco P. Andriulli
publish: IEEE Journal of Electromagnetics, RF and Microwaves in Medicine and Biology
abstract: A Nyström-based high-order (HO) discretization scheme for surface integral equations (SIEs) for analyzing the electroencephalography (EEG) forward problem is proposed in this work. We use HO surface elements and interpolation functions for the discretization of the interfaces of the head volume and the unknowns on the elements, respectively. The proposed scheme is more flexible and versatile than the existing isoparametric HO discretization schemes for the following reasons: The interpolation points are different from the mesh nodes, allowing for the flexible manipulation of the order of the basis functions without regenerating the mesh of the interfaces. Moreover, the interpolation points are chosen from the quadrature rules with the same number of points on the elements simplifying the numerical computation of the surface integrals for the far-interaction case. In addition, we extend the implementation of the HO discretization scheme to the double-layer and the adjoint double-layer formulations, as well as to the isolated-skull-approach for the double-layer formulation and to the indirect adjoint double-layer formulation, employed to improve the solution accuracy in case of high conductivity contrast models, which requires the development of different techniques for the singularity treatment. Numerical experiments are presented to demonstrate the accuracy, flexibility, and efficiency of the proposed scheme for the four SIEs for analyzing the EEG forward problem.

title: Fractional-Order Soft Actor Critic for Vision-Based UAV Navigation with Structured Experience Replay
authors: Tao Xu, Chaoyue Chen, Dong Liu
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: Deep reinforcement learning holds significant potential for continuous control tasks, but its real-world application is often limited by algorithmic instability and poor sample efficiency. Although Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) is a strong and widely adopted baseline for continuous control, its standard temperature adaptation and uniform replay mechanism may limit training stability and data utilization in complex vision-based navigation tasks. To overcome these limitations, we introduce the Fractional-order Temperature Adaptive Soft Actor-Critic (FSAC) framework. FSAC integrates two core innovations: a fractional-order temperature controller that uses Grünwald-Letnikov derivatives to incorporate historical gradient information, thereby stabilizing entropy tuning and damping oscillatory dynamics; and a quadrant-based experience replay (QER) strategy that jointly rank-normalizes transition Q-values and temporal-difference errors to construct balanced mini-batches, improving sample informativeness and distributional diversity without relying on hyperparameter-sensitive prioritization. Experiments on OpenAI Gym benchmarks show that FSAC improves stability and performance in most settings. Furthermore, when deployed with a curriculum learning strategy in high-fidelity AirSim simulations for unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) navigation across simple, dense, and dynamic obstacle environments, FSAC exhibits improved training stability and generalization performance, particularly in dynamic scenarios. These results indicate that FSAC provides a robust and efficient extension of SAC for complex, non-stationary control problems.

title: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems Information for Authors
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems

title: Differential Game-Based Event-Triggered Impulsive Control for Disturbed Dynamical Systems
authors: Wenlu Liu, Hangjun Che, Chuandong Li, Zhi-Hong Guan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics: Systems
abstract: This article investigates the game-based impulsive control problem for a class of dynamical systems subject to exogenous disturbances. Particularly, a novel Zeno-free event-triggered mechanism (ETM) related to Hamiltonian functions is designed to determine the impulse frequency for optimality and helps to build a bridge between impulsive control and differential game. Utilizing event-triggered impulsive control (ETIC) technique, the stability performance analysis and Nash equilibrium setting are synthesized under the framework of zero-sum impulse game. First, some sufficient conditions for achieving ${\mathcal {L}}_{2}$ stability are provided via transforming impulsive control issue into zero-sum impulse game. Second, the Nash equilibrium solution containing an optimal impulsive control strategy and worst case disturbance policy is obtained under ETIC method. Moreover, the stability is analyzed for the systems under the derived equilibrium strategies. Finally, the effectiveness of the constructed impulsive control scheme and equilibrium strategies is validated through substantial simulations on practical systems.

title: Explainable Multimodal Intelligence for Adaptive Identity Document Fraud Detection
authors: Sameer Sethi, Kinshuk Kumar, Parisha Khurana, Preeti Kaur
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Systems in Engineering, Secured Systems and Cybersecurity (ICISESSC)
abstract: Identity document verification systems are increasingly susceptible to complicated presentation attacks (PAs) such as copy-move fraud and facial morphing. The current visual-based deep learning methods are highly unreliable and difficult for explanation. In this paper we present a transparent multistage framework to identify identity document fraud from structured metadata and document images side-by-side. The architecture combines an image encoder based on EfficientNet with a metadata encoder using BiLSTM, which is connected by deep feature fusion. It is fine-tuned via a 2 -step finetuning scheme. Experimental results on the synthesized dataset demonstrate that our approach achieves better performance in all types of attacks with standard PAD metrics. Grad-CAMs also contribute to transparency and forensic interpretability for tooltips.

title: Study on the Influence of Different Liner Structures on Jetting Projectile Charge Forming and Penetration Performance
authors: Wenfeng Guo, Yuting Liu
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Perception and Autonomous Control (IPAC)
abstract: In order to optimize the design of shaped warhead and improve the effectiveness of anti-armor weapons, this paper explores the influence of three kinds of liner structures (with wall thickness as the core variable) of spherical shape, conical shape and cylinder-cone combined shape on the forming and penetration performance of Jetting Projectile Charge (JPC) through theoretical analysis and finite element numerical simulation. The results show that The spherical shaped liner with a radius of curvature of 50 mm only forms a complete JPC when the wall thickness is 1.6mm and 2.4mm. The conical shaped charge liner can form JPC in the range of wall thickness of 2.4 mm, 3.2 mm, 4.0 mm and 4.8 mm. Due to structural defects, only high-speed fracture jets are generated under all wall thicknesses, and JPC cannot be formed. In terms of penetration performance, two kinds of effective JPC have good penetration effect on concrete target, but there are differences in the penetration of steel target and aluminum alloy target. The penetration depth of JPC formed by spherical liner into steel, concrete and aluminum alloy targets is 90 mm, 218 mm and 141 mm, respectively. Compared with the steel target, the penetration effect of the concrete concrete target is increased by 140%, and the penetration effect of the aluminum alloy target is increased by 57%. The penetration depths of JPC formed by conical liners into steel, concrete and aluminum alloy targets are 57 mm, 184 mm and 126 mm, respectively. Compared with the steel target, the penetration effect of the concrete target is increased by 223%, and the penetration effect of the aluminum alloy target is increased by 132%. This study has certain reference significance for the study of the forming mechanism and armor breaking function of the shaped rod penetrator.

title: Microstructure Reconstruction of Porous Materials via a Conditional Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Model with Soft and Hard Data
authors: Hai Mei
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: Reconstruction of porous material microstructures is essential in fields like geology and materials science. Although deep learning has advanced this area, achieving high-quality and highly controllable reconstructions remains challenging. We propose a conditional soft- and hard-data denoising diffusion probabilistic model (CSHDDPM) that integrates soft and hard condition data into the multi-scale networks of diffusion models. This enables comprehensive feature understanding and generates diverse, high-quality results that satisfy condition constraints. Experiments on Berea and Beadpack datasets demonstrate that CSHDDPM outperforms other deep learning models in controllability and reconstruction quality.

title: DEEP: Differentiable Explicit Projection for Deep Networks with Unitary Constraints
authors: Tianxiong Chen, Xinyuan He, Pengyu Wang, Kanghua Xie, Wei He, Yingguan Wang
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: Maintaining stability in deep and recurrent neural networks is essential, yet gradient vanishing and explosion remain major challenges. In this paper, we introduce DEEP (Differentiable Explicit Projection), a framework that enforces unitary constraints on network weights through a fully differentiable explicit projection mechanism. DEEP leverages the Björck iteration to efficiently project unconstrained weights onto the unitary manifold, preserving norms and inner products while remaining fully compatible with backpropagation. Unlike previous implicit or computationally expensive methods, DEEP achieves strict orthogonality with minimal computational overhead. We evaluate DEEP across multiple benchmarks, including random unitary matrix learning, long-sequence Copying Tasks, pixel-by-pixel MNIST, and convolutional networks on CIFAR-10, demonstrating that a small number of Björck iterations is sufficient to maintain numerical stability, improve training convergence, and enhance overall performance. These results highlight DEEP as a practical and general approach for enforcing unitary constraints, offering an effective trade-off between stability, efficiency, and predictive accuracy in deep learning.

title: Fog-Edge Enabled Internet of Sound Framework for Integrated Consumer Healthcare Systems
authors: Syed Rizwan Hassan, KangYoon Lee
publish: IEEE Transactions on Consumer Electronics
abstract: The Internet of Sound (IoS) paradigm enables networking of sound devices for sensing, processing, and transmission of acoustic information. Integrating advanced acoustic sensor technologies and signal processing techniques can provide a suitable platform for implementing advanced applications. The phonocardiogram (PCG) has been extensively utilized in the diagnosis of coronary heart disease (CHD), one of the major causes of death. This research presents a novel Fog computing-enabled IoS-based (FIoS) framework that processes patients' heart sounds (HS) for real-time cardiac disease diagnosis. Furthermore, this research proposes a rate-aware node selection algorithm (RANSA) that accounts for edge nodes' sensing rates when connecting to fog devices. Finally, an adaptive resource-based clustering algorithm (ARBCA) is designed to provide efficient resource utilization in dynamic computing environments. Evaluations performed against the traditional FIoS baseline demonstrate that the proposed RANSA reduces latency by 7.22%, energy consumption by 0.82%, network consumption by 26.44%, and computational cost by 32.64%, while ARBCA reduces latency by 95.0%, energy consumption by 11.54%, network consumption by 78.69%, and computational cost by 77.4%, highlighting its optimum performance.

title: Infrared-Based Human Action Recognition Under Low-Light and Occlusion Conditions
authors: Aditya Balodi, Neeraj Kumar Pandey, Manoj Diwakar, Sanjay Roka, Aditya Joshi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Systems in Engineering, Secured Systems and Cybersecurity (ICISESSC)
abstract: The multimodal human action recognition framework being proposed in this study uses Infrared (IR) and skeletal motion clues along with a complementary fusion pipeline. The proposed system uses the ActiVit Transformer to get the high-level spatiotemporal features from the raw video sequences, taking advantage of its multi-head attention and hierarchical temporal aggregation to represent the long-range visual dependencies with reduced redundancy. At the same time, the skeleton sequences are being handled by the HPI-GCN-RP, a very accurate graph convolutional network that is augmented with relation-preserving topology modeling, and this makes it possible to capture joint interactions and structural motion patterns very well. The different representations created by the two modalities are mapped into a common latent space and then combined via a learnable feature integration module that has been specifically constructed to keep the cross-modal correspondence. The integrated representation is then used for the final action classification, which allows the system to take advantage of both appearance-based dynamics and pose-driven motion signatures. The results of the experiments confirm that this fused architecture is capable of stronger generalization and better discriminability when compared to single-modality baselines, especially in situations where complex motion, occlusion, or viewpoint variation is present.

title: A Deep Learning System Based on YOLO for Finding Insects in Precision Agriculture
authors: Anant Raj, Swati Vashisht, Sandeep, Bhavya Yadav, Saurav Kumar, Siddhant Singh Somvanshi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Systems in Engineering, Secured Systems and Cybersecurity (ICISESSC)
abstract: Managing pests on farms is an important part of Food security and modern ways to protect crops. This study introduces a YOLO-based deep learning framework for realtime agricultural pest detection utilizing the YOLOv8 architecture Roboflow exported an annotated pest dataset that had five pest categories: weevil, caterpillar, grasshopper, slug, and snail. The suggested system's mean Average Precision (mAP@0.5) for all pest classes is 0.952. The AP scores for each class range from 0.849 for caterpillars to 0.995 for snails and grasshoppers. To make sure that the framework works well in a variety of field situations, It uses data augmentation methods, decoupled anchor-free detection heads, and multi-scale feature pyramid networks. Tests on Google Colab's GPU infrastructure show that the system works much better than baseline models like YOLOv5 and Faster R-CNN, which can process more than 60 frames per second in real time with good accuracy and speed. Some uses are automated systems for monitoring crops, drones for field surveillance, and platforms for precision agriculture, and smart farms can get help and advice. Future research will focus on expanding the framework to include additional crop and pest species, along with more advanced YOLO variants and getting it to work on edge devices with limited resources.

title: Classification and Detection of Poultry Disease from Chicken Fecal Images Using Deep Learning Techniques
authors: Neduncheziyan, Marie Suja J, Amrita Vijay V, Esther Sheebaa S
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Systems in Engineering, Secured Systems and Cybersecurity (ICISESSC)
abstract: There are poultry diseases that present serious problems in terms of global food security and the poultry industry due to their impact on the economy and increased death rate. Detecting such conditions at an early stage and with high precision is very important for the prevention of further damage; however, traditional methods usually take too much time and lead to mistakes. This research suggests the use of deep learning to automatically diagnose various poultry diseases based on the image analysis of chicken feces. DenseNet-121 neural network model with SE blocks showed excellent results when applied to classification with 97.75% accuracy. Moreover, for disease localization, YOLOv8 model was used and provided high values of Mean Average Precision and Precision-recall. Both models have been implemented in Streamlit and Flask applications.

title: Research on Photovoltaic Energy Storage Cooperative Scheduling and Energy Management Strategies Based on Reinforcement Learning
authors: Peizhi Liu
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: When a high proportion of photovoltaic (PV) power is connected to the grid, its output fluctuates significantly, placing high pressure on energy storage regulation. Furthermore, rule-based control lacks sufficient adaptability. To address this, this paper proposes a PV-storage collaborative scheduling method based on reinforcement learning. The method treats the grid-connected PV-storage system as the research object, integrating PV output, load demand, electricity price signals, State of Charge (SOC), and prediction bias to form a time-series state. It utilizes time-series feature encoding, secure projection, and Lagrange constraint updates to achieve improvements in economy, stability, and security. Under the same experimental protocol, rule-based control, heuristic methods, and traditional deep reinforcement learning models are compared. Ablation experiments and perturbation studies are used to verify its effectiveness and robustness. Results show that this method performs better in terms of total operating cost, curtailment rate, peak-to-valley difference, and SOC violation rate. It can enhance PV absorption, achieve orderly charging and discharging of energy storage, and improve the smooth control capability of grid-connected power. This demonstrates that reinforcement learning can provide an effective intelligent energy operation approach for PV-storage collaborative scheduling. Ablation analysis shows that temporal encoding mostly enhances cost-related coordination, whereas safety prediction mostly guarantees feasibility; nonetheless, existing evidence has been restricted to a station-level simulated platform.

title: InfraShip-Net: An Efficient Deep Learning–Based Algorithm for Maritime Infrared Ship Detection
authors: Jiawei Liu
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: Accurate detection of maritime infrared ship targets is critical for maritime safety monitoring and autonomous maritime systems. However, ship targets in infrared maritime images often exhibit large scale variations, small object sizes, low contrast, and are easily affected by sea-surface reflections and environmental noise, making reliable detection challenging. To address these issues, this paper proposes InfraShip-Net, an improved YOLOv12-based detection framework. The model integrates a Multi-scale Semantic Marine Fusion Module (MSMF) for enhanced multi-level feature fusion, a Sea-Background Guided Attention mechanism (SBGA) to suppress sea clutter and highlight ship regions, and a Remote Weak-signal Enhancement Unit (RWEU) to improve detection of distant small ships. Experimental results on a public maritime infrared dataset demonstrate that InfraShip-Net achieves an mAP of 96.7% while maintaining real-time performance, significantly outperforming the baseline YOLOv12 model.

title: A CNN-BiLSTM Model with Attention and Gated Fusion for Uyghur Text Classification
authors: Jiahui Wang
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: Uyghur, as a typical low-resource agglutinative language, faces significant challenges in text classification, including limited annotated corpora, rich morphological variations, and difficulties in semantic modeling. To address these issues, we construct a 19,648-sample Uyghur news corpus with preprocessing steps including stemming and stop-word filtering, and learn word embeddings via Word2Vec.
