title: AG-STDF: Anatomy-Guided ECG Spatiotemporal Dynamical Fusion for Screening Coronary Microvascular Dysfunction_supp1-3698945.docx
authors: Qinghua Sun
publish: N/A
abstract: Objective: Coronary slow flow (CSF), present in 1-7% of coronary angiograms, occurs in patients with angiographically normal epicardial arteries and is associated with acute coronary syndrome, representing an unmet need in coronary microvascular dysfunction (CMD) management. This study proposes an anatomy-guided spatiotemporal dynamical fusion (AG-STDF) framework for CSF screening. Methods: AG-STDF integrates anatomical electrophysiological territory mapping with 12-lead electrocardiogram (ECG) dynamics. ECG signals are decomposed into coronary territories-left anterior descending (LAD), left circumflex (LCX), and right coronary artery (RCA)—and static morphology is fused with intrinsic dynamics (derived from dynamic modeling) using joint recurrence quantification and multi-scale decomposition. Results: AG-STDF achieves an AUROC of 0.9467 <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\pm$</tex-math></inline-formula> 0.0272 for CSF detection. At the vessel level, where therapeutic decisions are made per 2024 European Society of Cardiology guidelines, AG-STDF predicts corrected thrombolysis in myocardial infarction frame counts (CTFC) with an <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\text{R}^{\text{2}}$</tex-math></inline-formula> = 0.5951 <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\pm$</tex-math></inline-formula> 0.0666 (AUROC: 0.8428). Notably, AG-STDF performs steadily in right-dominant coronary patients, with favorable results in LAD (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\text{R}^{\text{2}}$</tex-math></inline-formula> = 0.6753) and mild fluctuations in LCX (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\text{R}^{\text{2}}$</tex-math></inline-formula> = 0.5673) and RCA (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\text{R}^{\text{2}}$</tex-math></inline-formula> = 0.5296) due to anatomical variations and competitive perfusion. Anatomical constraints and spatiotemporal fusion improve AUROC by 3.5% (p <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$< $</tex-math></inline-formula> 0.01) and 9.47% (p <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$< $</tex-math></inline-formula> 0.05). When equipped with an Intel Core i5-10500 and NVIDIA GTX 1660S, AG-STDF processes a 10-second ECG segment in 19.37 s. Conclusion: AG-STDF allows accurate, non-invasive CSF detection and vessel-level assessment in right-dominant populations. Significance: It serves as a cost-effective CMD screening tool that may reduce invasive testing and support early diagnosis.

title: Phoneme-Based Accent-Adaptive Speech Recognition for Accurate and Mispronunciation Feedback
authors: G. W. C. K. Fernando, A. I. Meegoda, L. A. D. Sanjana, D. G. S. Dilshan, M. P. Gunathilake, D. Kasthurirathna
publish: 2026 8th International Congress on Human-Computer Interaction, Optimization and Robotic Applications (ICHORA)
abstract: The increasing use of online meeting platforms has intensified the need for clear spoken communication, particularly among non-native English speakers. While modern Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) systems provide accurate transcription, they offer limited support for pronunciation assessment in spontaneous multi-speaker environments. This paper presents a hybrid accent-adaptive pronunciation detection framework for post-meeting analysis of South Asian English speech. The proposed system integrates commercial ASR transcription with a fine-tuned Whisper model to improve accent robustness, and combines word-level confidence scoring with phoneme-level alignment using the Montreal Forced Aligner and the CMU Pronouncing Dictionary. A hybrid decision mechanism incorporating confidence thresholds and phoneme deviation analysis is further enhanced with an accent-aware filtering strategy to reduce false positives. Experimental evaluation on multi-speaker meeting recordings demonstrates a significant reduction in false positive rate and improved pronunciation detection accuracy. The framework maintains computational efficiency suitable for post-meeting deployment while providing interpretable, sound-level diagnostic feedback. The results confirm that hybrid confidence-phoneme integration offers a practical and scalable solution for automated post-meeting pronunciation feedback in multi-speaker online meeting environments.

title: Dual-Channel LLM-Guided Semantic Feature Extraction with Zero-Shot Classification for Zero-Day Malware Detection
authors: Ahmed Essaa Al-Owaidi, Galip Cansever
publish: 2026 8th International Congress on Human-Computer Interaction, Optimization and Robotic Applications (ICHORA)
abstract: Zero-day malware remains a significant challenge for conventional detection systems due to its ability to evade signature-based models and exhibit previously unseen behavioral patterns. This paper proposes a dual-channel semantic detection framework guided by a large language model to address this challenge. The framework extracts complementary representations from static code artifacts and dynamic behavioral traces, where both channels are encoded using CodeBERT, a transformer-based model pre-trained on source code and programming-related text. The resulting semantic embeddings are fused into a unified representation that enables zero-shot classification of malware families without requiring prior exposure to the target class. The proposed method is evaluated on five publicly available datasets-Malware Dataset, Windows Malware Dataset, Network Malware Detection Dataset, CTU IoT Traffic, and IoT Botnet Traffic-under a strict family-exclusion protocol to simulate realistic zero-day conditions. Experimental results show that the framework achieves known-class detection accuracy ranging from 98.88% to 99.34%, while maintaining zero-day detection accuracy between 96.38% and 98.03%. Ablation studies demonstrate that the dual-channel design consistently outperforms single-channel variants, and comparisons with recent state-of-the-art approaches confirm improved robustness and generalization. These findings indicate that integrating code-level and behavior-level semantics through CodeBERTdriven fusion provides an effective and scalable strategy for detecting previously unseen malware families.

title: Design of an Asymmetric Converter for 6/4 Switched Reluctance Motor
authors: S. Allirani, B.S.Vaishnavi, S.M.Sawmiya, A.K.Sai Vasanth
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Trends in Electronics and Informatics (ICOEI)
abstract: Switched Reluctance Motors are commonly employed in electric vehicles and industrial drives because of their simplicity and fault tolerance. But the high torque ripple due to the non-linear magnetic core characteristics is a drawback that restricts the performance. This paper proposes the design and simulation study of an asymmetric bridge converter for a 6/4 Switched Reluctance Motor with the objective of torque ripple reduction. A closed-loop control system incorporating PI and PID controllers is developed and simulated using MATLAB/Simulink. The torque performance is studied before and after employing the proposed control method. The simulation study reveals that the application of PID control has a substantial effect on torque ripple reduction and torque smoothness compared to the PI control method.

title: Automated Brain Tumor Segmentation in Multi-Parametric MRI using nnU-Net
authors: D.Ganesh, P.Venkateswarlu Reddy, Neeruganti Vidya Sree, Thotla Chakra Ganesh, Pudi Nikhil Surya Teja, Galam Dhanunjaneya
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Trends in Electronics and Informatics (ICOEI)
abstract: Accurate brain tumor segmentation from MRI forms an integral part of diagnosis, treatment planning, and disease monitoring. Manual segmentation is really time-consuming and prone to inter-observer variability, hence motivating the need for automated solutions. This paper reports a fully automated deep learning-based framework for brain tumor segmentation using nnU-Net for multi-parametric MRI data: T1, T1-contrast enhanced (T1-CE), T2, and FLAIR modalities. The idea borrowed the self-configuring capability of nnU-Net, meaning it automatically adjusts network architecture, preprocessing, and strategies for training based on dataset characteristics, without requiring manual tuning of hyperparameters. The model was evaluated on the BraTS dataset and achieved Dice similarity coefficients of 0.92 for whole tumor (WT), 0.89 for tumor core (TC), and 0.87 for enhancing tumor (ET). Experimental results on accurate boundary delineation and strong generalization capability have shown improved performances compared to conventionally used U-Net-based approaches. The proposed approach provides a robust, reproducible solution for automated brain tumor segmentation and is thus prospective in computer-aided diagnosis integrations into clinical environments.

title: When to Fly: A Passenger Decision-Support System Based on Climate-Sensitive Flight Disruption Likelihood
authors: Nasim Ahmadzadeh Nobari Azar, Mohammad Karami
publish: 2026 8th International Congress on Human-Computer Interaction, Optimization and Robotic Applications (ICHORA)
abstract: Flight status is significantly impacted by adverse weather and poor air quality conditions, which in turn increase the likelihood of disruptions and cancellations. Despite this, most airline booking systems do not explicitly estimate the risk associated with weather or air-quality forecasts and do not inform travelers. This study introduces a climate-sensitive scheduling framework designed to recommend safer departure times, alternative routing options, or short delays to help reduce disruption risk in the airline industry for travellers. To develop and test the framework, the flight-level data were collected from the U.S. Bureau of Transportation Statistics (BTS) for the JFK-LAX route in January 2024, combined with hourly meteorological and air-quality data from Open-Meteo. A gradient-boosted model was then trained to estimate the probability of flight disruptions. The results point to daily “no-fly window” that reflects how weather and air-quality conditions increase the likelihood of flight cancellations. This tool is to support travelers at the booking stage, guiding them to choose flights with lower risk and avoid missed connections. It is especially useful for patients traveling internationally, where disruptions can delay urgent medical care. This study provides insights from different managerial perspectives.

title: Performance Evaluation of Machine Learning, Deep Learning and Ensemble Approaches in Diabetes Prediction
authors: Swarnali Daw, Bingshati Mondal, Devadeepa Chakraborty, Suparna Paul, Ishika Halder, Bidyajani Maji
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Emerging Trends in Advancements and Applications of Computational Intelligence Techniques (ETAACT)
abstract: Diabetes mellitus has emerged as one of the most alarming health concerns all over the world, indicating the importance of error-free and early detection systems. Here, we present a relative study of multiple Machine Learning and Deep learning algorithms applied to the Pima Indian Diabetes dataset. Traditional classifiers, including K-Nearest Neighbors, Support Vector Classifier, Random Forest, and AdaBoost, were evaluated with SMOTE and cross-validation. The Deep Neural Network (DNN) is combined with Explainable AI (XAI) methods. The inclusion of ensemble learning approaches can elevate predictive performance by integrating the strengths of different models. In this paper, the above three models are implemented, and their ability to predict diabetic patients is relatively studied based on accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score. A comparison table is developed to show these results .Through this comparative study we have observed that Random Forest demonstrates the topmost level of accuracy. These findings would guide future research efforts in Diabetes diagnosis.

title: Speech-to-Sign Language Translator
authors: Dinky Khurana, Jasmine Kaur, Baishnavi Patsani, Amit Vajpayee
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Emerging Trends in Advancements and Applications of Computational Intelligence Techniques (ETAACT)
abstract: Polysemy between the hearing-impaired and the hearing-impaired people has been another great challenge considering that there is very little real-time interpretation of the sign language. Current speech-to-sign translation systems are characterized by a very long latency, limited vocabulary and poor contextual awareness. The current paper suggests a speech to sign language translator that works in real-time and gains its effectiveness results on the basis of Natural Language Processing (NLP) and deep learning methods to increase its accuracy and responsiveness. The suggested system takes the form of initially translating verbal information into text through an automatic speech recognition component and then analyzes the meaning of the utterances with NLP to avoid a loss of context. The model is then a deep learning program to map the processed text into the sign language representation. The system will also aid in the scalability of the vocabulary and effective generation of gestures to be able to provide a more fluent and natural generation of signs. Experimental analysis shows that it is more accurate in translation and the time of response is less than the traditional approaches. The suggested system is a workable and scalable model of inclusive communication and may be used effectively in an educational, healthcare, and public service setting.
